FullText: The birth genesis : origin beginning of Jesus Christ was as follows : His mother Mary had been promised to Joseph in marriage . But before they were married , Mary realized that she was pregnant by Holy Spirit .
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('had been promised'), and the lexical verb 'promise' licenses both an external argument (agent: implied subject performing the promise) and an internal argument (patient: 'Mary', the one promised), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('had been promised'), and the lexical verb 'promise' licenses an external argument (the agent who made the promise, suppressed in passive voice), thus failing the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had been promised' licenses an internal argument (Mary as the theme/patient receiving the promise), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event with an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'had been promised' denotes a punctual speech act or commitment that constitutes a boundary transition into a betrothal state, lacking a durative process phase leading to completion.
AktAch_explanation: The passive construction 'had been promised' denotes a durative state of betrothal or commitment rather than a punctual transition into a new state; the event unfolds over measurable duration and does not represent a single temporal boundary crossing.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had been promised' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the completion of a promise agreement, with an inherent endpoint and result state (Mary being in a promised state), not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had been promised' entails a resulting state change (Mary transitioning into a state of being betrothed to Joseph), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had been promised' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect (had been + -ed participle in passive voice), which entails dynamic eventive unfolding and is incompatible with stative readings per the set_to_0_if criteria.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'had been promised to Joseph in marriage' uses past perfect to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (Mary's betrothal occurred before the subsequent events), not to indicate a counterfactual situation; the event actually occurred in the narrative world.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been promised' is not negated; the sentence affirms that Mary was promised to Joseph, with no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never', or negative quantifier) directly modifying this eventuality.
DtRes_explanation: The stative copular construction 'had been promised' describes a state rather than encoding a transition; it reports a past condition without indicating that a resulting state persists at the reference time, especially given the subsequent clause 'But before they were married' which indicates the promise was not yet fulfilled.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (the act of promising Mary to Joseph in marriage) rather than a situation holding continuously over time; the focus is on the transition into a betrothal state, not on duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'Mary had been promised to Joseph in marriage' presents a bounded passive transition event (the act of promising/betrothal as a completed whole) rather than an ongoing state, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for passive bounded transitions.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been promised' asserts a realized past fact (Mary was betrothed to Joseph) without modal, conditional, counterfactual, or future marking, and does not occur in a desire or hypothetical context.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had been promised' contains the morphologically past auxiliary 'had' combined with 'been' and a past participle, instantiating both past perfect and progressive marking, which satisfies the set_to_1_if criteria.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been promised' instantiates VcPass: it has the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of transitive verb 'promised', the grammatical subject 'Mary' is the semantic patient (the one promised), and the passive morphology is overt.

FullText: When Joseph woke up , he did what the angel of God had commanded him to do . He took Mary to be his wife .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had commanded' introduces an overt external argument (the angel of God) and an overt internal argument (him), satisfying the two-participant transitive pattern with both subject and direct object present.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'commanded' licenses an external argument (the angel as agent/initiator) and introduces an internal argument (him as the object of the command), satisfying the set_to_0_if criterion that the verb licenses an external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'commanded' licenses an internal argument (the infinitival complement 'him to do'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'commanded' denotes a punctual speech act that establishes a directive at a single moment, lacking a durative process phase or inherent endpoint that defines completion of the commanding itself.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had commanded' denotes a punctual transition into a directive state (the angel issuing a command at a specific moment), which is a cognitive/communicative achievement that establishes a new result state, satisfying the criteria for AktAch.

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had commanded' entails a resulting state (a command that Joseph must obey) and does not constitute a single punctual occurrence without consequent result, thus it is not semelfactive.

ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'had commanded' expresses temporal anteriority within a narrative (the angel's command preceded Joseph's action), describing an event that actually occurred in the story world, without any indication of counterfactuality.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'did' applies to the matrix clause 'he did what...', not to the embedded target gram 'had commanded'; the angel's commanding is presented as realized, not negated.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had commanded' describes a directive (change of social/legal status or binding instruction) that remains in effect at the reference time, as evidenced by Joseph's subsequent compliance with the command in the narrative.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'had commanded him to do' describes a discrete past event (the angel's command) that Joseph then executed, not a situation holding continuously from a past point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had commanded' presents a completed transition event (the angel's act of commanding) as a bounded whole prior to Joseph's action, fitting the perfective construal of a discrete, bounded occurrence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had commanded' is a past perfect form asserting a realized fact in the actual world (the angel had previously given a command that Joseph then obeyed), with no modal, conditional, counterfactual, or future marking.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had commanded' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'the angel of God' as the semantic agent (doer) and 'him' as the grammatical object (semantic patient); it lacks the passive morphology 'have/had + been + past participle'.

FullText: » Repent , for the kingdom of heaven has come near . «

ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has come near' is not negated; the sentence is a positive assertion that the kingdom of heaven has come near, with no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never', or negative quantifier) modifying it.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'come near' describes a change of location/state where the kingdom of heaven has moved to a proximate position and remains near at the reference time, satisfying the resultative condition that the resulting state holds currently.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'has come near' denotes a transition into a new state (the kingdom moving from distant to near) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and there is no duration adverbial indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'come near' is a transition event construed as a completed whole, presenting the kingdom of heaven's approach as a single bounded occurrence rather than an ongoing process.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has come near' is a present perfect that asserts the kingdom of heaven as having arrived in the actual world, not as projected, hypothetical, or desired; it is a realized fact presented without modal, conditional, or future marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has come near' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'come' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'has come near' is active voice with an intransitive verb 'come'; it lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle' and the grammatical subject 'kingdom of heaven' is the semantic agent/experiencer, not the patient.

FullText: Then a voice from heaven said : » This is my Son whom I love and whom I have approved . «

ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a situation that has actually occurred in the actual world (a voice from heaven stating an approval that has taken place), without any indication of counterfactual meaning, conditional structure, or unfulfilled wish.

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have approved' describes a change of social/legal status (approval granted to the Son) that is currently held at the reference time, as indicated by the present-tense context and the divine declaration that this approval is in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'whom I have approved' describes a discrete bounded event (an act of approval) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, with no duration adverbials or habitual iteration indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have approved' denotes a stative situation (approval as a state of favor or endorsement) without a construed boundary or transition event, and the clause does not present a bounded occurrence but rather an ongoing state of approval.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have approved' is asserted as a realized fact within direct speech from a divine voice, presenting the approval as an actual state of affairs in the world of the text, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.

VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have approved' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'I' as the semantic agent (the one doing the approving) and the object 'whom' as the semantic patient; it lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: Jesus heard that John had been arrested . So Jesus withdrew to Galilee .
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive and the lexical verb 'arrest' licenses both an external argument (agent) and an internal argument (patient/John), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('had been arrested'), and the lexical verb 'arrest' licenses an external argument (the agent who performed the arrest), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for passive clauses.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'arrested' licenses an internal argument (John functions as the theme/patient of the arrest action), and the construction is passive voice where the sole overt argument is the internal argument rather than an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: Arrest denotes a punctual boundary transition (the moment someone is taken into custody) without a durative process phase, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'arrested' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from freedom to custody) that occurs at a definable temporal boundary, satisfying the achievement criteria despite the past perfect tense marking.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'arrest' entails a specific, inherent endpoint (the completion of the arrest action) and a defined result state (being in custody), making it a bounded accomplishment rather than a dynamic, durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had been arrested' entails a transition into a new resulting state (the state of being arrested/imprisoned), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had been arrested' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect ('had been + -ed'), which entails dynamic eventive unfolding and change-of-state (the transition from free to arrested), incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'had been arrested' presents a factual past event (John's arrest actually occurred) and uses past perfect to express temporal anteriority within a narrative, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'arrested' describes a change of state (from free to detained), and the construction 'had been arrested' indicates that John remains in the arrested state at the reference time when Jesus heard the news.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (John's arrest) rather than a situation holding continuously over time; the focus is on the transition into the state of being arrested, not on a duration or habitual series.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'had been arrested' is a passive form asserting a bounded transition event (arrest as a completed whole) that serves as a discrete narrative step triggering the subsequent action ('So Jesus withdrew'), meeting the criteria for TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been arrested' asserts a realized fact in the actual world (John's arrest as a past event that occurred before Jesus heard about it), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary is morphologically past ('had') and the gram includes 'been' followed by a past participle ('arrested'), matching both the past auxiliary criterion and the progressive perfect criterion.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been arrested' instantiates VcPass: it has the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of transitive verb 'arrest', the grammatical subject 'John' is the semantic patient (entity affected by arrest), and overt passive morphology is present.

DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event of hearing/receiving information rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.

FullText: » Do not loudly announce it when you give to the poor . The hypocrites do this in the houses of worship and on the streets . They do this to be praised by men . Believe me , they have already been paid in full .

FullText: » Do not pray like the hypocrites ! They want everyone to see them in the houses of worship and on the street corners . I assure you they have been paid in full .

AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have gone through' denotes a durative process of traversing multiple cities over measurable time rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and naturally permits the completion frame 'finish going through all the cities.'
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'have gone through all the cities' entails a specific, inherent endpoint (completing passage through all cities), making it a bounded accomplishment rather than a durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have gone through all the cities' entails a transition into a resulting state (completion of traversing cities) and unfolds over measurable duration rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have gone through' denotes a dynamic change-of-state event involving internal progression (traversing cities sequentially), which entails eventive unfolding and bounded completion rather than a static condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'before you have gone through all the cities of Israel' presents a future temporal condition within an open conditional context ('I tell you'), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have gone through' is not negated; the negation ('before') is a temporal conjunction indicating the event has not yet occurred at a future reference point, but the gram itself is not syntactically negated with 'not', 'n't', a negative quantifier, or 'never'.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'before you have gone through all the cities of Israel' uses a duration/completion adverbial ('all the cities') in a conditional temporal frame, indicating an ongoing process rather than a resulting state that persists at reference time; the focus is on the completion of the action itself, not a resulting state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (completing passage through all cities) rather than a continuous situation holding over time; the focus is on the transition/completion of going through cities, not on a state or activity extending to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have gone through' construes 'going through all the cities' as a bounded transition/completion event that must occur before another event (the Son of man coming), presenting it as a single bounded whole rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have gone through' appears in a temporal clause ('before you have gone through all the cities') that projects a future completion; it is embedded within a larger prediction ('the Son of man will come'), presenting the situation as hypothetical and not yet realized.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have gone through' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle 'gone through', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition.

FullText: » From the days of John the Baptist until now , the kingdom of heaven has been man’s goal , and men of violence have attempted to take it by force .
ASTrns_explanation: The passive construction 'has been [man's goal]' licenses both an external argument (the kingdom of heaven as the affected entity) and an internal argument (man's goal as the predicative complement), meeting the passive criterion for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: This is a copular clause with 'be' where the sole argument 'the kingdom of heaven' functions as the syntactic subject linked to a predicative complement 'man's goal'; the verb does not license an external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target 'has been' is a copular verb that licenses an internal argument (the predicate nominal 'man's goal'), not an unergative verb with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'has been' denotes a stative condition (the kingdom being a goal) without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it describes a persistent state rather than an accomplishment with inherent completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb phrase 'has been' denotes a stative condition (the kingdom being a goal) that unfolds over an extended duration from the past until now, rather than a punctual transition into a new state.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'has been' in 'the kingdom of heaven has been man's goal' denotes a stative situation with no dynamic unfolding or sustained engagement; it expresses a state of being rather than an activity, lacking the dynamic, durative character required for AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'has been' is a stative perfect construction expressing a resulting state that persists from past to present, not a punctual dynamic occurrence without change of state.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has been' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect, which entails dynamic unfolding and is explicitly incompatible with stative readings per the set_to_0_if criteria.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world (the kingdom of heaven has been man's goal from the days of John the Baptist until now), without any indication that it is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has been' is not negated; it appears in a positive assertion that the kingdom of heaven has been man's goal, with no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never') or negative quantifier modifying it.
DtRes_explanation: The stative copula 'has been' describes an ongoing state ('man's goal') rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state; per edge case rules, copular constructions do not encode change of state even if the condition resulted from prior change.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'the kingdom of heaven has been man's goal' contains the duration adverbial 'from the days of John the Baptist until now' indicating a state that extends continuously from a past starting point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'has been man's goal' presents a stative situation (being a goal) without a construed boundary or transition, and the duration adverbial 'from the days of John the Baptist until now' makes the internal temporal span accessible rather than packaging it as a bounded whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has been' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (the kingdom of heaven has functioned as man's goal from the past until now), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has been' includes 'been' followed by a progressive participle context (the kingdom of heaven has been man's goal), which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for progressive perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the noun phrase 'man's goal' (a non-verbal predicate), making 'been' a lexical copula rather than a passive auxiliary; passive voice requires 'been' + past participle of a transitive verb.

FullText: » From the days of John the Baptist until now , the kingdom of heaven has been man’s goal , and men of violence have attempted to take it by force .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'attempted' licenses an external argument (agent 'men of violence') and introduces a direct object ('to take it'), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria for verbs that license external arguments and contain overt objects.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'attempted' licenses an internal argument (the infinitival complement 'to take it by force'), which means it does not instantiate ASUnrg, which requires no internal argument.

AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have attempted' denotes an ongoing or repeated effort to achieve a goal rather than a punctual transition into a new state; it describes a durative process of trying that unfolds over time and can be interrupted, not a boundary-crossing achievement.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have attempted to take it by force' denotes a bounded, goal-oriented situation with an inherent endpoint (successfully or unsuccessfully taking the kingdom by force), not a dynamic durative activity without defined completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have attempted to take it by force' entails a transition into a resulting state (the attempt itself as a completed action with consequences) and is construed as a durative activity rather than a single punctual occurrence, thus not semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have attempted' denotes a dynamic activity involving volitional engagement and eventive unfolding (the act of trying to take the kingdom by force), which lacks the static, unchanging quality required for AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents 'have attempted' as a factual situation that has actually occurred in the actual world (men of violence have indeed attempted to take the kingdom by force), with no indication of counterfactuality, unfulfilled wishes, or contrary-to-fact conditions.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have attempted' is not negated; the negation does not apply to this verb phrase but rather to the broader context, and the clause itself presents the event as occurring (men of violence have attempted to take the kingdom by force).
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'attempt' describes an activity or effort without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; the clause reports a past action without indicating that a change of state currently holds.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'men of violence have attempted to take it by force' is part of a coordinated structure with 'the kingdom of heaven has been man's goal' spanning 'from the days of John the Baptist until now', describing a repeated/habitual series of attempts that characterizes the entire time span from past to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have attempted' construes 'attempt' as a transition event (a discrete action with a construed endpoint) asserted as a completed whole within a narrative sequence describing historical events, meeting the criteria for perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have attempted' is in present perfect form asserting a realized historical fact (men of violence have actually attempted to take the kingdom by force), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have attempted' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have attempted' is active voice with 'men of violence' as the grammatical subject (semantic agent) and 'to take it by force' as the complement; it lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: » Woe to you , Chorazin ! Woe to you , Bethsaida ! If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon that were done in you , they would have repented long ago in sackcloth and ashes .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'had been done' is a passive construction that licenses both an external argument (implied agent performing the mighty works) and an internal argument (the mighty works as patient), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('had been done'), and the lexical verb 'do' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent performing the mighty works), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for passive clauses.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target verb 'had been done' is a passive construction where the logical subject (the mighty works) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing the action rather than as an external argument (agent/initiator), thus failing the ASUnrg criterion.
AktAcc_explanation: The passive construction 'had been done' denotes a punctual boundary transition into a completed state without a durative process phase; the event itself is instantaneous (the performance/execution of mighty works), not a gradual unfolding toward a goal.
AktAch_explanation: The passive construction 'had been done' denotes a completed durative process (the performance of mighty works) rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and the event unfolds over measurable duration rather than occurring at a single temporal boundary.
AktAct_explanation: The passive construction 'had been done' denotes a bounded, completed event with an inherent endpoint (the accomplishment of specific mighty works), not a dynamic durative activity without inherent completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had been done' entails a resulting change of state (the completion and establishment of mighty works in a location), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had been done' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect (past perfect progressive), which entails dynamic eventive unfolding and is incompatible with stative readings; additionally, the verb 'do' in this context denotes a bounded dynamic event (the performance of mighty works), not a static condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'had been done' occurs in the protasis of a counterfactual conditional ('If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon...') paired with the apodosis 'they would have repented', expressing a situation contrary to fact—the works were not done in those cities, only in the addressees' cities.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been done' appears in a conditional clause ('If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon') where no negator directly negates this verb; the negation structure is counterfactual/conditional rather than a direct semantic negation of the eventuality.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a stative copula construction (past participle in passive voice functioning as an adjective) that describes a state rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state that persists at the reference time; moreover, it appears in a counterfactual conditional clause ('If...would have') that negates the actual occurrence of the event.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a hypothetical discrete event (the performance of mighty works in Tyre and Sidon) rather than a situation holding continuously over time; it is a counterfactual conditional focused on a bounded occurrence, not a continuous state or habitual series.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'had been done in Tyre and Sidon' appears in a counterfactual conditional (if-clause) with 'would have repented', which construes the situation as hypothetical and temporally overlapping with another situation rather than as a discrete bounded event asserted in narrative sequence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been done' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('If the mighty works had been done...they would have repented'), presenting a hypothetical, non-actual situation contrary to fact.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had been done' contains the past-tense auxiliary 'had' followed by 'been' and a past participle, instantiating both past perfect and progressive perfect marking, which satisfies the set_to_1_if criteria.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been done' instantiates the passive voice: it has the form auxiliary 'have' (past tense: 'had') + 'been' + past participle 'done' of a transitive verb, with the grammatical subject 'the mighty works' functioning as the semantic patient (the entity affected by the event).

FullText: » Woe to you , Chorazin ! Woe to you , Bethsaida ! If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon that were done in you , they would have repented long ago in sackcloth and ashes .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'repented' does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it is used intransitively without an affected patient being syntactically realized.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'repented' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'they' who perform the action of repenting), making it unergative rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'repented' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('they') functioning as an external argument (agent engaged in a volitional activity) with no internal argument licensed or realized.
AktAcc_explanation: Repent denotes a punctual change-of-state (transition into a state of contrition) rather than a durative process with an inherent endpoint; the adverbial 'long ago' marks when the transition would have occurred, not a bounded duration of the repenting event itself.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'repent' denotes a punctual transition into a new mental/spiritual state (change from unrepentant to repentant), which is a definable threshold transition that can occur at a specific temporal boundary, meeting the criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'repent' denotes a bounded cognitive/emotional event marking a change of mind or state with an inherent endpoint (the achievement of repentance), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'repented' entails a transition into a new resulting state (a change from unrepentant to repentant condition), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'repent' denotes a change-of-state event involving internal dynamic development and a shift in condition, which is incompatible with AktState; the counterfactual conditional structure does not alter the inherently eventive nature of the verb.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'would have repented' occurs in the apodosis (result clause) of a counterfactual conditional introduced by 'If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon...', expressing an unfulfilled past consequence contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'would have repented' is not negated; it appears in a counterfactual conditional expressing what would have happened if conditions were different, but the gram itself contains no negator, negative quantifier, or 'never'.
DtRes_explanation: The target is a counterfactual conditional ('would have repented') expressing an unrealized hypothetical scenario, not an actual change of state that holds at the reference time; the resulting state does not persist in reality.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete, bounded event (repentance as a transition into a new state) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and the counterfactual conditional structure focuses on the occurrence of the event itself, not its duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'would have repented' construes 'repent' (a transition event) as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint in a counterfactual conditional, presenting the situation as a completed whole rather than ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'would have repented' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('If the mighty works had been done... they would have repented'), presenting the situation as hypothetical and unrealized rather than asserted as fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'would have repented' includes the modal auxiliary 'would' preceding 'have', which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for modal perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would have repented' is active voice with a grammatical subject 'they' functioning as the semantic agent (the doers of the repenting action), not a passive construction with the required 'have been + past participle' morphology.

FullText: » Will you , Capernaum , be exalted into heaven ? You will go down into the grave . If the mighty works had been done in Sodom , which were done in you , it would have remained until this day .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'had been done' is a passive construction that licenses both an external argument (agent, unexpressed) and an internal argument (patient 'mighty works'), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a passive construction where the lexical verb 'do' licenses an external argument (the agent performing the mighty works), which is suppressed in the passive voice, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for passive clauses.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target verb 'had been done' is a passive construction where the sole argument (the mighty works) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing the action, not as an external argument (agent/initiator), thus failing the ASUnrg criterion.
AktAcc_explanation: The passive construction 'had been done' denotes a punctual completion of an action (the performance of mighty works) without emphasizing a durative process phase; it marks a boundary transition into a completed state rather than a gradual, measurable unfolding toward a goal.

AktAct_explanation: The passive construction 'had been done' denotes a bounded, completed event with an inherent endpoint (the completion of the mighty works), not a dynamic durative activity without inherent completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The past perfect passive 'had been done' entails a resulting change of state (the completion and effects of mighty works), not a single punctual occurrence without consequences.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had been done' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect (past perfect progressive), which entails dynamic eventive unfolding and is incompatible with stative readings per the set_to_0_if criteria.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'had been done' occurs in the protasis of a counterfactual conditional ('If the mighty works had been done in Sodom...it would have remained'), where the condition is understood not to have been satisfied, indicating a situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been done' appears in a counterfactual conditional clause ('If the mighty works had been done in Sodom') where negation applies to the entire hypothetical scenario, not to the auxiliary itself; no negator directly negates the auxiliary or the eventuality expressed by the target gram.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a stative copula construction (past participle in copular form) that describes a state rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state that persists at the reference time; it appears in a counterfactual conditional clause ('If the mighty works had been done...') which negates the actual occurrence of the event.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a hypothetical discrete event (the performance of mighty works in Sodom) rather than a situation holding continuously over time; the focus is on the conditional occurrence of bounded actions, not on duration or habituality.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The passive form 'had been done' presents a bounded transition event (the performance of mighty works) as a completed whole in a counterfactual conditional, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for passive bounded transitions.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been done' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('If the mighty works had been done in Sodom...it would have remained'), presenting a hypothetical, non-actual situation rather than a realized fact.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had been done' contains the morphologically past auxiliary 'had' followed by 'been' and a past participle, instantiating both past perfect and progressive perfect features.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been done' instantiates the passive voice with the auxiliary sequence 'had' + 'been' + past participle 'done' of a transitive verb, where the semantic patient (the mighty works) functions as the grammatical subject.

FullText: » Will you , Capernaum , be exalted into heaven ? You will go down into the grave . If the mighty works had been done in Sodom , which were done in you , it would have remained until this day .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'remained' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it only takes an external argument (the implied subject 'it') and functions as a stative verb without affecting a patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'remained' denotes a state of existence/persistence where the sole argument (Sodom, implied) functions as an internal argument (theme), and the verb does not license an external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'remained' denotes a change-of-state or stative condition where the sole argument (Sodom) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing or experiencing a state, not as an external argument initiating an action.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'remain' denotes a stative condition without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it describes a continuous state rather than an accomplishment with inherent completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'remained' denotes a durative state of persistence over a measurable duration ('until this day'), not a punctual transition into a new state, and fails all set_to_1 criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'would have remained' denotes a state (remaining in existence) with an inherent temporal boundary ('until this day'), indicating a bounded situation with a defined endpoint rather than a dynamic, durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'would have remained' entails a resulting state (Sodom remaining standing until the present day) and unfolds over measurable duration ('until this day'), not a single punctual occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The conditional perfect 'would have remained' denotes a durative state of persistence over time without dynamic change or internal eventive unfolding, satisfying the stative criterion of a situation that holds over time with no inherent endpoint.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'would have remained' occurs in the apodosis of a counterfactual conditional ('If the mighty works had been done in Sodom...it would have remained'), expressing a result that did not occur because the condition was not satisfied.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'would have remained' is not negated; the negation in the conditional clause ('If the mighty works had been done') applies to the antecedent condition, not to the eventuality of remaining.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'would have remained' is a stative copula describing a hypothetical continuing state rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state; it does not entail a change of state that holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'it would have remained until this day' contains the duration adverbial 'until this day' and denotes a non-terminating state (remaining) that would have held continuously from a past starting point to the reference time, satisfying the criteria for DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'remain' denotes a stative situation without a construed boundary, and the conditional counterfactual construction presents an ongoing state rather than a bounded transition or completion.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'would have remained' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('If the mighty works had been done in Sodom...it would have remained'), presenting a hypothetical situation contrary to fact rather than a realized state.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'would have remained' includes the modal auxiliary 'would' preceding 'have', which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for modal perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would have remained' is a conditional perfect form with 'remained' as a non-verbal predicate (state/condition) rather than a passive past participle; 'have' is the perfect auxiliary, not 'have been' + past participle, so passive morphology is absent.

FullText: Then Jesus prayed : » I thank you , O Father , Lord of heaven and earth . You have hidden these things from the wise and understanding , and have revealed them to children .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have hidden' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('You') and an overt direct object ('these things'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have hidden' licenses an external argument (You/God as agent) and introduces a direct object ('these things'), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria for agentive verbs with overt objects.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have hidden' licenses an internal argument ('these things'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have hidden' denotes a punctual change-of-state (transition from revealed to hidden) without a durative process phase, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have hidden' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (concealment from perception) and can be modified by a specific time-point adverbial (e.g., 'You have hidden these things at that moment'), meeting the criteria for an achievement verb.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have hidden' denotes a completed action with a defined result state (things are now hidden as a consequence), lacks dynamic durative unfolding, and cannot naturally be modified by 'for X time' without semantic oddity, matching the set_to_0_if criteria for bounded events with inherent endpoints.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have hidden' entails a resulting change of state (things are now hidden as a consequence), which disqualifies it from being semelfactive according to the set_to_0_if rule.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have hidden' denotes a change-of-state event (concealment) involving dynamic progression from a state of visibility to hiddenness, which is incompatible with AktState's requirement for situations lacking internal eventive unfolding.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'You have hidden these things' presents an action understood to have actually occurred in the actual world (a past action with present relevance), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have hidden' is not negated; it appears in a positive declarative statement where Jesus thanks the Father for having hidden things from the wise, with no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never', or negative quantifier) directly negating this verb.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have hidden' describes a change of state (concealment) where things are currently in a hidden state at the reference time, and the parallel structure with 'have revealed' reinforces that both resulting states (hidden and revealed) persist in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'have hidden' denotes a completed action of concealment (a transition into a state of hiddenness) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual markers indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have hidden' is a transition event (hide = to cause something to become hidden) construed as a completed whole, presenting a bounded occurrence rather than an ongoing or stative situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have hidden' is a present perfect form asserting a realized action in the actual world within Jesus's prayer of thanksgiving, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have hidden' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have hidden' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'You' as the semantic agent (doer) and 'these things' as the grammatical object (semantic patient); it lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: Then Jesus prayed : » I thank you , O Father , Lord of heaven and earth . You have hidden these things from the wise and understanding , and have revealed them to children .

AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have revealed' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous change-of-state (transition from hidden to revealed) without a durative process phase, matching the achievement category rather than accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have revealed' denotes a punctual transition into a new state of disclosure/awareness (making something known that was previously hidden), which is a definable threshold event that does not unfold over measurable duration and can be modified by specific time-point adverbials.

AktState_explanation: The verb 'have revealed' denotes a change-of-state event (making something known that was previously hidden) involving dynamic progression from a state of concealment to disclosure, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'have revealed them to children' presents an actual occurrence within Jesus's prayer of thanksgiving, describing a factual situation in the actual world without any counterfactual marking or indication that the event did not occur.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have revealed' is not negated; it appears in a positive coordinate clause parallel to 'have hidden' and expresses an event that is realized rather than absent.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have revealed' describes a change of state (from hidden to revealed) where the resulting state of disclosure to children is currently in effect at the reference time, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for change of state with persisting result.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'have revealed' denotes a discrete bounded event (the action of revealing) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, with no duration adverbials or habitual framing present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'reveal' is a transition event (moving from hidden to revealed state), and 'have revealed' presents this transition as a completed bounded whole, asserting that the action of revealing has been accomplished.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have revealed' is a present perfect form asserting a realized action in the actual world within Jesus's prayer of thanksgiving, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have revealed' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have revealed' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'You' as the semantic agent (doer) and 'them' as the grammatical object (semantic patient); it lacks the passive morphology 'have been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: » All things have been delivered to me from my Father . Only the Father knows the Son , and only the Son knows the Father . The Son tells others about the Father so that they can know him too .
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('have been delivered'), and the lexical verb licenses both an external argument (agent: 'my Father') and an internal argument (patient: 'All things'), satisfying the passive condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('have been delivered'), and the lexical verb 'deliver' licenses an external argument (the Father, suppressed in the passive construction), thus failing the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target verb 'have been delivered' is passive voice with 'things' functioning as an internal argument (theme/patient) receiving the action, not as an external argument (agent/initiator), thus failing the ASUnrg criterion.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'delivered' denotes a punctual boundary transition (transfer of possession from Father to Son) without a durative process phase, fitting the achievement category rather than accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'delivered' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (transfer of possession/control completed at a definable boundary), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'delivered at that moment'), and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying the achievement criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have been delivered' denotes a completed transfer with an inherent endpoint and result state (things are now in a state of having been delivered), satisfying the set_to_0_if criterion of entailing a specific, defined result state.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have been delivered' entails a resulting change of state (things transitioning from not-delivered to delivered), which disqualifies it from being semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have been delivered' is morphologically marked with progressive/perfect aspect ('have been') indicating a completed dynamic event of transfer, which involves change-of-state and eventive unfolding incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a situation as having actually occurred in the actual world ('All things have been delivered to me'), with no indication of counterfactuality, unfulfilled wishes, or conditional reasoning.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have been delivered' is in a positive declarative statement with no negator (not, n't, never, or negative quantifier) negating it; the sentence affirms that things have been delivered.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have been delivered' describes a change of possession (things transferred from Father to Son), and the resulting state of possession is currently in effect at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a completed transfer of discrete entities (things) from the Father to the speaker, focusing on the resultant state rather than a situation holding continuously over time, which aligns with the set_to_0 criterion for transitions into new states.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'have been delivered' presents a passive transition event (delivery as a completed bounded occurrence) with a construed endpoint, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for passive bounded transitions.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have been delivered' is in the present perfect tense asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world (things have already been delivered to the speaker), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have been delivered' includes 'been' followed by a past participle in a progressive-like construction, which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for progressive perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have been delivered' instantiates VcPass: it has the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of transitive verb 'delivered', the grammatical subject 'All things' is the semantic patient (entity affected), and the agent is expressed in a 'by'-phrase ('from my Father').

FullText: » If you had known what this means , ‘ I desire mercy and not sacrifice , ’ ’ you would not have condemned the guiltless .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had known' introduces an overt internal argument ('what this means'), satisfying the requirement for a two-participant transitive construction with an external argument (you) and an internal argument (the content clause).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'know' licenses an external argument (the subject 'you' as the knower/agent), making it unergative rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had known' licenses an internal argument (the clause 'what this means'), making it a transitive verb that does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'had known' denotes a punctual cognitive transition into a state of awareness or understanding, lacking a durative process phase directed toward a defined endpoint, and thus instantiates an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had known' denotes a state of awareness that unfolds over measurable duration rather than a punctual transition into a new state; knowing is a durative cognitive state, not an instantaneous crossing of a threshold.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had known' denotes a bounded cognitive state with a defined onset point (the moment of acquiring knowledge), lacks dynamic unfolding, and does not require sustained dynamic engagement, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for bounded perceptual/cognitive events marking the onset of awareness.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had known' entails a resulting state of knowledge and unfolds over a duration rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, thus it does not instantiate the semelfactive feature.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had known' denotes a mental state (knowledge) that would have held over time in the counterfactual condition, with no dynamic change or eventive unfolding, matching the stative feature criteria.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target 'had known' occurs in the protasis of a counterfactual conditional ('If you had known...') paired with the apodosis 'you would not have condemned', indicating a situation contrary to fact where the condition was not satisfied.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'not' in the sentence applies to 'have condemned' (the consequent clause), not to the target gram 'had known' in the conditional antecedent clause.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had known' is in a counterfactual conditional clause ('If you had known...') that negates the actual occurrence of the event; no transition to a resulting state is entailed in the real world at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a counterfactual conditional with a past perfect verb expressing a hypothetical state at a single point in time, not a situation extending continuously to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials or iterative markers required for DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'had known' denotes a stative situation (knowledge/cognition) without a construed boundary or transition, and the clause does not present a bounded occurrence but rather a hypothetical state that would have overlapped with the conditional consequence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had known' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('If you had known...you would not have condemned'), which presents the situation as hypothetical and non-actual rather than realized.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary is morphologically past ('had' + past participle 'known'), which instantiates the past perfect form rather than the simple present perfect, satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had known' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'you' as the semantic agent (doer) and lacks the passive morphology 'have/had + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: » If you had known what this means , ‘ I desire mercy and not sacrifice , ’ ’ you would not have condemned the guiltless .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'condemned' introduces an overt grammatical subject (you) and an overt direct object (the guiltless), satisfying the transitive pattern required for ASTrns=1.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'condemned' licenses an external argument (the subject 'you' as agent) and introduces a direct object ('the guiltless'), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria for verbs that license external arguments and contain overt grammatical objects.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'condemned' licenses an internal argument ('the guiltless' as direct object), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'condemn' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous boundary transition (moving from a state of not-condemned to condemned) without a durative process phase, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for achievement verbs.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'condemned' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the crossing of a threshold from non-condemnation to condemnation), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials ('you would not have condemned them at that moment'), and does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase, satisfying the criteria for AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'condemned' denotes a bounded, punctual event with an inherent endpoint (the act of condemning someone) and a defined result state (the person being condemned), which does not instantiate a dynamic, durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'condemned' entails a transition into a resulting state (the guiltless becoming condemned), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'condemned' denotes a bounded dynamic event involving a change-of-state (transitioning from not-condemned to condemned), which is incompatible with AktState; the conditional perfect construction emphasizes the eventive nature of this action.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'would not have condemned' occurs in the apodosis (result clause) of a counterfactual conditional introduced by 'If you had known,' expressing an unfulfilled past action contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The auxiliary 'would' of the target gram 'would not have condemned' is directly negated by 'not', satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The target is a counterfactual conditional (would not have condemned) that describes a hypothetical past event that did not occur; it does not entail an actual change of state or resulting state holding at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'would not have condemned' describes a discrete bounded event (the act of condemning) that would not occur, not a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'would not have condemned' is a counterfactual conditional with 'have' + past participle (condemned), presenting the situation as a bounded transition event (condemning) that would have been completed as a single whole, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for transition events construed as completed wholes.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'would not have condemned' contains the modal auxiliary 'would' in a conditional construction ('If you had known...you would not have condemned'), presenting the situation as counterfactual and hypothetical rather than realized.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'would not have condemned' includes the modal auxiliary 'would' preceding 'have', which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for modal perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would not have condemned' is active voice with a grammatical object ('the guiltless'), not passive voice; it lacks the required 'been' + past participle structure and the subject 'you' is the semantic agent, not the patient.

FullText: » Look ! My servant whom I have chosen . I am well pleased with him . I will put my Spirit upon him and he will declare judgment to the nations .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have chosen' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('I') and an overt direct object ('whom'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns=1.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'chosen' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent performing the selection), and the clause contains an overt direct object 'whom' (relative pronoun referring to 'servant'), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have chosen' licenses an internal argument (the direct object 'whom'), therefore it does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have chosen' denotes a punctual boundary transition into a selected state without a durative process phase; it cannot naturally be modified by 'in X time' to measure completion duration, and represents an instantaneous decision-event rather than a gradual culminating process.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have chosen' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (selection of the servant) that can be modified by a specific time-point adverbial and does not permit interruption, satisfying the criteria for an achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have chosen' denotes a completed action with an inherent endpoint (the selection of the servant) and a defined result state (the servant being chosen), which does not instantiate a dynamic, durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have chosen' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not-chosen to chosen status) and does not constitute a single punctual occurrence without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have chosen' denotes a change-of-state event (selection/decision) involving dynamic progression from a state of non-selection to selection, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'whom I have chosen' presents a situation that has actually occurred in the actual world (the servant has been chosen), with no indication of counterfactuality, contrary-to-fact condition, or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have chosen' is not negated; it appears in a positive relative clause describing the servant, and no negator (not, n't, never, or negative quantifier) directly negates this verb.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have chosen' describes a change of status (selection/appointment), and the context 'My servant whom I have chosen' indicates that the chosen status currently holds at the reference time, satisfying the resultative condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'whom I have chosen' describes a discrete, completed action of selection rather than a situation holding continuously over time, with no duration adverbials or iterative series present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have chosen' is a transition event (selection as a bounded occurrence) presented as a completed whole, asserting a discrete bounded event rather than an ongoing or stative situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have chosen' is in the present perfect tense asserting a realized fact in the actual world (the servant has already been chosen), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have chosen' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have chosen' is active voice (subject 'I' is the semantic agent performing the action of choosing), not passive voice; passive voice requires the form 'have been chosen' with the subject as semantic patient.

FullText: » But if I cast out demons by God’s Spirit , then the kingdom of God has come to you .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has come' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); 'to you' is a prepositional phrase indicating direction, not a grammatical object.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument 'the kingdom of God' undergoes displacement with no external argument licensed, matching the unaccusative pattern.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a change-of-location/appearance event where the sole argument 'the kingdom of God' functions as an internal argument (theme) undergoing a change of state rather than as an external argument initiating an action.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual boundary transition (arrival at a location or state) without a durative process phase, matching the achievement category exemplified by 'arrive'.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival/presence of the kingdom), naturally permits modification by specific time-point adverbials (e.g., 'has come at that moment'), and represents a boundary crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual event with an inherent endpoint (arrival at a destination) and a defined result state (the kingdom being present), not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has come' entails a transition into a new resulting state (the kingdom of God arriving/being present), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a change-of-state event with a clear boundary marking the arrival/onset of the kingdom of God, involving dynamic progression from a prior state to a new state, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'the kingdom of God has come to you' presents a situation as actually occurring in the actual world (the kingdom has arrived as a consequence of casting out demons), not as contrary to fact, and occurs in the apodosis of an open conditional where the protasis condition is presented as a real possibility.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has come' is not negated; it appears in a positive conditional clause asserting that the kingdom of God has come, with no negator ('not', 'n't', negative quantifier, or 'never') directly negating the target gram.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'come' describes a transition to a destination ('to you'), and the context indicates the kingdom of God has arrived and is currently present at the reference time, satisfying the resultative condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a transition into a new state (arrival of the kingdom) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'come' is a transition event (arrival/occurrence), and 'has come' presents this transition as a completed bounded whole with a construed endpoint, meeting the criterion for perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has come' appears in the consequent of a conditional construction ('if...then'), where the entire clause expresses a hypothetical or projected outcome dependent on the condition, rather than asserting a realized fact in the actual world.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has come' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'come', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has come' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); 'come' is a past participle used in active perfect aspect with 'has', and the grammatical subject 'kingdom of God' is the semantic agent/experiencer, not the patient.

FullText: » Have you understood all these things ? « They said to him : » Yes . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'understood' introduces an overt internal argument ('all these things'), satisfying the criterion for a two-participant transitive event with an external argument (you) and an internal argument (patient/theme).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'understand' licenses an external argument (the subject 'you' as agent/experiencer) and takes a direct object ('all these things'), therefore it does not meet the criteria for unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'understood' licenses an internal argument ('all these things' as direct object), therefore it does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'understand' denotes a punctual cognitive transition into awareness or comprehension without a durative process phase; it is an instantaneous change-of-state that marks entry into a mental state rather than a gradual culmination toward a defined endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'understood' denotes a punctual cognitive transition into awareness or recognition (comprehension of things previously unclear), which is a near-instantaneous crossing of a definable threshold from non-understanding to understanding, meeting the criteria for AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'understood' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of awareness or comprehension, which is treated as a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have understood' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not understanding to understanding) and is not a punctual dynamic occurrence without consequent result, thus it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The present perfect 'have understood' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the completion of understanding (onset of awareness), which is a change-of-state from not understanding to understanding, thus encoding eventive change rather than a static stative condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause is an interrogative question asking about actual comprehension in the present/immediate context, not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish, and presents the situation as a genuine inquiry about what has actually been understood.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have understood' is in a yes/no question with no negator (not, n't, never, or negative quantifier) directly negating it; the question is positive in form.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'understand' describes a cognitive change of state (from not understanding to understanding), and the question presupposes that the resulting state of comprehension currently holds at the reference time, as confirmed by the affirmative response 'Yes.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (understanding/comprehension at a point in time) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual iteration markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'Have you understood all these things?' presents understanding as a bounded, completed cognitive transition with a construed endpoint, asking whether the addressee has achieved comprehension of the material, which instantiates TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The interrogative form 'Have you understood' seeks confirmation of an actual, realized state of affairs in the real world (the disciples' comprehension), not a projected or hypothetical situation; interrogative form alone does not trigger irrealis.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have understood' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have understood' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); it is active voice with 'you' as the semantic agent and grammatical subject.

FullText: And he said to them : » Every scribe journalist writer who has been made a disciple to the kingdom of heaven is like a man that is a householder , who brings out of his treasure things new and old . «
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been made'), and the lexical verb 'make' licenses both an external argument (agent, unexpressed) and an internal argument (patient: 'a disciple'), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been made'), and the lexical verb 'make' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent who makes someone a disciple), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for passive clauses.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has been made' is passive and licenses an internal argument ('a disciple'), making it transitive; the subject 'who' functions as a theme/patient undergoing the action rather than as an external agent/initiator.
AktAcc_explanation: The passive construction 'has been made a disciple' denotes a punctual transition into a state (becoming a disciple) rather than a durative process with an inherent endpoint, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has been made a disciple' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (becoming a disciple), representing a definable threshold crossing that lacks measurable internal duration and can be modified by specific time-point adverbials (e.g., 'was made a disciple at that moment').
AktAct_explanation: The passive construction 'has been made a disciple' denotes a bounded event with an inherent endpoint (the completion of becoming a disciple) and a defined result state, not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The passive construction 'has been made a disciple' entails a resulting change of state (transition from non-disciple to disciple), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has been made' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect ('has been' + past participle), which entails dynamic change and eventive unfolding incompatible with stative readings; additionally, 'made' denotes a change-of-state event (becoming a disciple).
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'who has been made a disciple to the kingdom of heaven' presents a situation as actually occurring within the narrative world without any counterfactual marking, temporal anteriority context, or indication that the event did not occur.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has been made' is not negated; no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never') directly modifies it, and no negative quantifier negates the eventuality it expresses.
DtRes_explanation: The construction 'has been made a disciple' is a stative copular passive that describes a resulting state rather than encoding a transition; it reports the current status without emphasizing the change into that state, and the clause does not entail that a resulting state from a prior change persists at the reference time in the sense required by DtRes.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (the transition of becoming a disciple) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and contains no duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'has been made a disciple' is a passive form asserting a bounded transition event (becoming a disciple) as a completed whole, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for passive bounded transitions.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has been made' asserts a realized state of affairs (a scribe who has become a disciple) as a fact within the actual world of the discourse, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has been made' includes 'been' followed by a progressive/passive participle 'made', which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for progressive perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the NP 'a disciple', a non-verbal predicate functioning as a copular complement, not a past participle in passive voice; 'been' here is the lexical copula, not a passive auxiliary.

FullText: For John had said to him : » It is not lawful for you to have her . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had said' introduces an overt internal argument (the direct object clause 'It is not lawful for you to have her'), satisfying the transitive requirement of ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'said' licenses an external argument (John as agent/speaker) and introduces an indirect object ('to him'), thus failing the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'said' licenses an internal argument (the indirect object 'to him' and the direct object clause 'It is not lawful...'), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'had said' denotes a punctual speech act that constitutes a boundary transition into a completed utterance without a durative process phase, matching the achievement profile rather than accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had said' denotes a punctual transition into a new state of communication (the utterance of speech), which is a boundary event that can be modified by specific time-point adverbials and does not permit interruption as an ongoing process phase.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had said' denotes a single, punctual speech act with a defined completion point (the utterance was completed), not a dynamic durative situation; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without changing meaning, and it lacks the homogeneous property required for activities.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had said' entails a transition into a resulting state (the utterance has been made and exists as a completed communicative act with consequences), and the past perfect construes it as a durative speech act rather than a single punctual occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had said' denotes a bounded dynamic event (speech act with clear onset and completion), which involves eventive unfolding and change, triggering the set_to_0_if criterion for change-of-state and dynamic activity.
