ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had cut out of' is not negated; the clause describes a completed action (he cut the tomb out of rock) as a realized event, with no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never') or negative quantifier modifying it.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'cut out' describes a change of state (rock transformed from uncut to cut/carved), and the resulting state (a tomb carved from rock) persists at the reference time as the location where the body is laid.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'which he had cut out of the rock' describes a discrete, completed action (the cutting of the tomb) rather than a situation holding continuously over time; it focuses on the transition/result of the action, not on duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had cut out of' presents the action of cutting as a completed, bounded transition event that occurred prior to the main narrative events, functioning as a discrete step in the narrative sequence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had cut out of' is a past perfect form asserting a realized action in the actual world (he completed the action of cutting the tomb), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past tense, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the set_to_1_if condition for past auxiliary marking.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'had cut out of' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'he' as the semantic agent (doer) and lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle'; 'cut' is a past participle functioning as part of a perfect active construction, not a passive.

FullText: The angel spoke to the women : » I know you seek Jesus who has been impaled ; do not fear .
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive with 'has been impaled', and the lexical verb 'impale' licenses both an external argument (agent, unexpressed) and an internal argument (patient, realized as the subject 'Jesus'), satisfying the passive condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been impaled'), and the lexical verb 'impale' licenses an external argument (an agent who performed the impaling), even though it is not overtly expressed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'impaled' licenses an internal argument (Jesus as the theme/patient undergoing the change-of-state of being pierced), and the passive construction indicates the sole argument functions as an internal argument rather than an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'impale' denotes a punctual, near-instantaneous boundary transition (piercing/penetrating) without a durative process phase, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'impale' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (being pierced/affixed), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'was impaled at dawn'), and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying the achievement criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'impale' denotes a bounded, telic event with an inherent endpoint (the state of being impaled), lacks dynamic durative unfolding, and does not satisfy the 'for X time' test without implying an ongoing process rather than a completed result state.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has been impaled' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not-impaled to impaled condition), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has been impaled' is marked with progressive aspect (present perfect continuous form) and denotes a bounded change-of-state event (the action of being impaled), both of which are incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'who has been impaled' presents a factual situation that actually occurred in the narrative world (Jesus was impaled), not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has been impaled' is not negated; the negation 'do not fear' in the sentence applies to a different clause and does not negate the eventuality of being impaled.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has been impaled' describes a change of state (from not-impaled to impaled) where the resulting state (being impaled) persists at the reference time, meeting the criterion for DtRes.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has been impaled' denotes a punctual transition into a resultant state (the action of impalement and its completion) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the negative example 'I have broken the vase.'
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'has been impaled' is a passive form asserting a bounded transition event (impalement as a completed whole) rather than an ongoing process, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for passive bounded transitions.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has been impaled' is embedded in a relative clause that asserts a realized fact about Jesus in the actual world; it is not presented as projected, hypothetical, or uncertain, and no modal, conditional, future, or wish context applies.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has been impaled' includes 'been' followed by a progressive participle ('impaled'), matching the set_to_1_if criterion for progressive perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has been impaled' instantiates VcPass: it has the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of transitive verb 'impale', the grammatical subject 'Jesus' is the semantic patient (entity affected by impalement), and overt passive morphology is present.

FullText: » He is not here , for he has risen just as he promised . Come ; see the place where the Lord lay .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has risen' is intransitive with no overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it describes a change of state/position without an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes a change-of-location/directed motion event where the sole argument 'he' functions as an internal argument (theme) undergoing displacement, with no external argument licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes a change-of-location event where the sole argument (he) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing a change of state, not as an external argument initiating an action.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a punctual boundary transition from a state of being dead/laid to a state of being risen, without a durative process phase; it is an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from dead to alive/resurrected), lacks measurable internal duration, and represents a definable threshold crossing that does not permit interruption.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a bounded change of state with an inherent endpoint (transition from lying to risen position) and a defined result state, not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has risen' entails a transition into a new resulting state (from being dead/laid to being risen), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a change-of-state event (from dead to alive/resurrected) involving dynamic progression and an event boundary, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'he has risen' presents a situation understood to have actually occurred in the actual world (the resurrection is asserted as fact), not as contrary to fact, and contains no counterfactual marking.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negator 'not' in 'is not here' negates the copula 'is', not the target gram 'has risen'; the negation does not apply to the eventuality expressed by 'has risen'.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'risen' describes a change of location (from lying to risen), but the context 'He is not here' indicates the mover has departed from the reference location, so no resulting state of being at a destination persists at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from dead to risen) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and the adverb 'just' emphasizes the recent completion of this discrete event.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has risen' is a transition event (change of state from dead to alive) construed as a completed whole, and the clause functions as a discrete bounded occurrence in the narrative sequence, asserting the transition itself as a single completed event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has risen' asserts a realized fact in the actual world (He has risen = it is true that he rose), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present; negation of the main clause does not affect the realis status of the embedded clause.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has risen' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'risen', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has risen' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of intransitive 'rise'), not passive voice; the grammatical subject 'he' is the semantic agent (the one who rose), and there is no passive morphology ('have' + 'been' + past participle).

FullText: » Go quickly and tell his disciples he has risen from the dead . See , he goes before you to Galilee . You will see him there , as I have told you . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has risen' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); the prepositional phrase 'from the dead' is an adjunct, not a grammatical object.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes a change-of-state/directed motion event where the sole argument 'he' functions as an internal argument (theme) undergoing displacement from death to life, with no external argument licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes a change-of-location/change-of-state event where the sole argument (he) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing a transition from dead to alive, not as an external argument initiating an action.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a punctual boundary transition into a new state (from dead to alive) without a durative process phase; it is a near-instantaneous change-of-state rather than a gradual, goal-directed process.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from dead to alive), lacks measurable internal duration, and represents a definable threshold crossing that cannot be interrupted or partially completed.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of a defined result state (transition from dead to alive), with an inherent endpoint, not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has risen from the dead' entails a transition into a new resulting state (from dead to alive), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a change-of-state event (from dead to alive) involving a bounded dynamic transition with an inherent endpoint, which violates the stative requirement of lacking internal eventive unfolding.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents 'has risen from the dead' as an actual event that has occurred in the world of the narrative, not as a counterfactual or contrary-to-fact situation.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has risen' is not negated; it presents the event as occurring positively, with no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never') or negative quantifier modifying it.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has risen' describes a change of state (from dead to alive) and the context indicates the resulting state persists at the reference time, as evidenced by the subsequent statement 'he goes before you to Galilee' describing his current presence.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from dead to alive) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for transitions.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has risen' is a transition event (change of state from dead to alive) construed as a completed whole, presenting the situation as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has risen' asserts a realized fact in the actual world within the narrative context, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish markers present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has risen' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'risen', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'he' is the semantic agent (the one who rises/undergoes the change), and 'risen' is an intransitive past participle without the passive auxiliary sequence 'have + been + past participle of a transitive verb'; this is an active voice construction with present perfect aspect, not passive voice.

FullText: » Go quickly and tell his disciples he has risen from the dead . See , he goes before you to Galilee . You will see him there , as I have told you . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have told' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('I') and an overt direct object ('you'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'told' licenses an external argument (the speaker 'I') and introduces a direct object ('you'), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the clause contains an overt direct object introduced by the target verb.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'told' licenses an internal argument (the implied object 'you' receives the information), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have told' denotes a punctual speech act that is completed at a single moment of utterance without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it is a boundary transition into the state of having communicated information, not a gradual change-of-state or directed process toward completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have told' denotes a punctual transition into a new state of communication (the moment of utterance when information was conveyed to the addressee), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials ('I told you at that moment'), and does not permit interruption as an ongoing process phase.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have told' denotes a completed communicative act with a defined result state (the disciples have been informed), lacks dynamic durative unfolding, and is a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of awareness, fitting the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have told' entails a resulting state (the disciples now possess the information that was communicated) and does not constitute a single punctual occurrence without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have told' denotes a bounded perceptual/communicative event marking the completion of a past speech act, which is eventive change rather than a static condition holding over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'as I have told you' presents a situation that actually occurred in the past (a prior telling), without any indication that it is contrary to fact or unfulfilled.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have told' is not negated; the negator 'not' does not appear in the clause, and no negative quantifiers or 'never' modify it.
DtRes_explanation: The communication verb 'have told' merely reports a past interaction (prior instruction) without entailing that the information is currently binding or in effect; the clause does not indicate a persisting directive state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'as I have told you' describes a discrete past communicative event (the act of telling) rather than a situation holding continuously from a past point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have told' denotes a stative situation (knowing/informing) without a construed boundary or transition; the clause does not present a bounded completion but rather an unbounded state of having communicated information.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have told' asserts a realized past action in the actual world ('as I have told you'), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have told' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'told' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have told' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'I' as the semantic agent and the object 'you' as the semantic patient; it lacks the passive morphology 'have been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: Jesus came to them and spoke to them , saying : » All authority has been given to me in heaven and on earth .
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been given'), and the lexical verb 'give' licenses both an external argument (agent, unexpressed) and an internal argument (patient: 'All authority'), satisfying the passive condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been given'), and the lexical verb 'give' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent who gave the authority), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for passive clauses.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has been given' is passive and licenses an internal argument ('All authority'), making it transitive; the grammatical subject functions as a theme/patient receiving the action rather than as an external agent/initiator.
AktAcc_explanation: The passive construction 'has been given' denotes a punctual transfer or bestowal of authority that constitutes a boundary transition into a state of possession, lacking a durative process phase leading to completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has been given' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (authority transferred to Jesus) that can be modified by a specific time-point adverbial and does not permit interruption, satisfying the achievement criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The passive construction 'has been given' denotes a bounded event with a specific result state (authority transferred to Jesus), not a dynamic durative activity; it entails completion and cannot naturally take 'for X time' modification without implying the transfer is ongoing.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has been given' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not having authority to having authority), which disqualifies it from being a semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has been given' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect (present perfect continuous structure), which entails dynamic eventive unfolding and change-of-state (the transfer/bestowal of authority), incompatible with stative readings per set_to_0_if criteria.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'All authority has been given to me' presents a situation as actually occurring in the actual world (Jesus stating a fact about his authority), with no indication of counterfactuality, unfulfilled wishes, or conditional reasoning.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has been given' is not negated; the sentence presents the event as occurring (authority has been given), and no negator applies to this verb.
DtRes_explanation: The passive construction 'has been given' describes a change of possession/authority where the resulting state (Jesus possessing all authority) persists at the reference time, meeting the criterion for resultative aspect.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'has been given' denotes a transition into a new state (the conferral of authority) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and there is no duration adverbial indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The passive form 'has been given' presents a bounded transition event (the granting of authority) as a completed whole, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for passive bounded transitions.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has been given' is a present perfect passive asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world (authority has been given to Jesus), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has been given' includes 'been' followed by a progressive/passive participle, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for progressive perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has been given' instantiates the passive voice: it has the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of the transitive verb 'give', the grammatical subject 'All authority' is the semantic patient (the entity affected), and the agent is omitted.

FullText: » Teach them to observe all the things I have commanded you . Behold , I am with you always , even to the end of the age . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have commanded' introduces an overt grammatical subject (I) and an overt direct object (you), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'commanded' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'I') and introduces a direct object ('all the things'), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria for verbs with external arguments and overt objects.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'commanded' licenses an internal argument ('you'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have commanded' denotes a punctual speech act that establishes a directive at a single moment of utterance, lacking a durative process phase or inherent endpoint that defines completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have commanded' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the issuance of commands as discrete speech acts that establish obligations), which can be modified by specific time-point adverbials and does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have commanded' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event with a specific result state (commands issued and received), not a dynamic durative activity; it entails completion of discrete communicative acts rather than ongoing homogeneous action.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have commanded' entails a resulting state (the commands now exist as binding directives) and is construed as a completed action with consequent result conditions, not a single punctual occurrence without change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have commanded' denotes a bounded dynamic event (issuing commands) involving change-of-state and internal eventive unfolding, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have commanded you' presents a situation that has actually occurred in the actual world (the speaker has genuinely issued commands), with no indication of counterfactuality, contrary-to-fact conditions, or unfulfilled wishes.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have commanded' is not negated; no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never') or negative quantifier modifies it, and the sentence presents the commanding as a realized event.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have commanded' describes a change of social/legal status (issuing binding directives) where the commands remain currently in effect as instructions to be observed, satisfying the set_to_1_if criterion for communication verbs with persisting directive force.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'have commanded' denotes a completed action of issuing commands in the past; the focus is on the discrete set of things commanded rather than a situation holding continuously from a past point to the reference time, and there is no duration adverbial indicating continuous extension.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have commanded' presents a completed transition event (the act of commanding) as a bounded whole, with the past participle 'commanded' construing the transition itself as a single accomplished occurrence rather than an ongoing or stative situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have commanded' is a present perfect form asserting a realized action in the actual world (commands that have already been given), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have commanded' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have commanded' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'I' as the semantic agent and 'you' as the grammatical object (semantic patient); it lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: Jesus said : » The time has come . The kingdom of God is at hand . Repent and believe the good news . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has come' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it only has an overt subject ('The time') with no affected patient realized syntactically.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument 'The time' undergoes displacement as an internal argument (theme), and no external argument is licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a change-of-location or appearance event where the sole argument 'time' functions as an internal argument (theme) rather than an external argument, matching the unaccusative pattern in set_to_0_if.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual boundary transition into a state (the arrival/occurrence of 'the time') without a durative process phase, matching the achievement category.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the arrival/occurrence of a specific time), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'has come at this moment'), and represents a definable threshold crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of a specific temporal state (the arrival of 'the time'), with an inherent endpoint and defined result state, not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has come' entails a transition into a new resulting state (the time has arrived/is now present), which disqualifies it from being a semelfactive; it describes a change of state rather than a punctual occurrence without consequence.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a change-of-state event marking the arrival/onset of a temporal boundary ('the time has come'), which involves dynamic change and eventive unfolding rather than a static condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'The time has come' presents a situation as actually occurring in the actual world (the kingdom of God is at hand), with no indication of counterfactuality, unfulfilled wishes, or conditional reasoning.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has come' is not negated; it expresses a positive assertion that the time has arrived, with no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never') or negative quantifier modifying it.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'come' describes a change of state (the time has arrived/reached the reference point) and the resulting state (the time being present/at hand) is currently in effect, as confirmed by the immediately following clause 'The kingdom of God is at hand.'
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the arrival of a moment in time) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, matching the negative example 'I have broken the vase.'
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'come' is a transition event, and 'has come' presents the arrival of 'the time' as a single bounded, completed occurrence rather than an ongoing process.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has come' is a present perfect that asserts the time as a realized fact in the actual world within Jesus's direct speech, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has come' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'come', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The form 'has come' is auxiliary 'have' + past participle, but 'come' is an intransitive verb with no passive morphology (no 'been'), and the subject 'The time' is the semantic agent/experiencer, not a patient; this is active voice, not passive voice.

FullText: » What do you want with us Jesus of Nazareth ? Have you come to destroy us ? I know who you are . You are the Holy One of God ! «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have come' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument; the infinitive phrase 'to destroy us' expresses purpose rather than functioning as a direct object of 'come'.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'you' who performs the action of coming with the purpose to destroy), making it unergative rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' licenses an internal argument (the infinitival complement 'to destroy us'), making it a transitive construction that does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a punctual boundary transition (arrival at a location/state) without a durative process phase; the infinitive 'to destroy' expresses purpose rather than constituting the event's internal temporal structure.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival at a location/moment), naturally modified by temporal boundaries, and does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'have come (to destroy)' denotes a bounded purposive event with an inherent endpoint (arrival at a location to accomplish a specific goal), not a dynamic durative activity without inherent completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The infinitive 'to destroy' entails a resulting change of state (transition from existence to destruction), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status regardless of the perfective 'have come' framing.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a bounded dynamic event marking the onset of arrival with purposive direction (to destroy), involving change-of-state and eventive unfolding rather than a static condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'Have you come to destroy us?' is an interrogative question about an actual or imminent situation, not a counterfactual conditional; it presents a possible real-world scenario without marking it as contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have come' is not negated; the negation marker does not appear in the clause containing this verb, and no negative quantifiers or 'never' modify it.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'come' is a motion verb, but the clause expresses a future-oriented purpose ('to destroy us') rather than indicating that the mover has arrived and remains at a destination with a persisting resulting state at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'Have you come to destroy us?' describes a discrete bounded event (arrival with purposive intent) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and contains no duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'come' is a transition event construed as a completed whole in a narrative sequence (direct speech questioning whether Jesus has arrived/come), presenting a bounded occurrence rather than an ongoing or stative situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have come (to destroy)' appears in an interrogative clause ('Have you come to destroy us?') that projects a hypothetical purpose or outcome rather than asserting a realized fact, triggering irrealis marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have come' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'come' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect pattern that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have come' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'you' as the semantic agent (the one performing the action of coming); there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle), and the subject is not the semantic patient.

FullText: He got up , took the bed and went out before them all . They were all filled with wonder . They gave glory to God and said we have never seen anything like this .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'seen' introduces an overt grammatical subject 'we' and an overt direct object 'anything', satisfying the condition for ASTrns marking.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'seen' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'we') and takes a direct object ('anything like this'), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the clause contains an overt direct object introduced by the target verb.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'seen' licenses an internal argument ('anything like this'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'see' in 'have never seen' denotes a punctual perceptual event—a single instantaneous transition into awareness—without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, matching the achievement (0) classification for cognitive/perceptual verbs construed as instantaneous transitions.
AktAch_explanation: The verb phrase 'have never seen' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (seeing/perceiving something for the first time), which is a cognitive-perceptual achievement that marks a boundary point of recognition rather than a durative process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb phrase 'have never seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness of a specific phenomenon ('anything like this'), which is a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have never seen' entails a resulting state of knowledge/experience and unfolds over an extended duration (a lifetime of not seeing), not a single punctual occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have never seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (the moment of perceiving something), which encodes eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering set_to_0_if criterion 4.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'we have never seen anything like this' presents a factual statement about the actual world (the witnesses genuinely have not seen such a thing), not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish, and contains no markers of contrary-to-fact meaning.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have never seen' is directly negated by the adverb 'never', which modifies the gram and presents the event as not occurring, matching the set_to_1_if criterion.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'see' is a perception verb that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; the clause reports a past experiential fact without a change of state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'we have never seen anything like this' describes a discrete bounded event (the absence of a prior experience) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and lacks duration adverbials indicating continuous extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have never seen' is a stative predicate denoting a persistent state of non-experience without a construed boundary or transition, and the negation 'never' profiles the absence of an occurrence rather than a bounded completed event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have never seen' asserts a realized state in the actual world (a fact about what the speakers have experienced), with negation alone not triggering irrealis; it is embedded in reported speech but still functions as a realis assertion of fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have never seen' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have never seen' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'we' as the semantic agent (doer) and 'anything' as the object (patient); it lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: Jesus heard this . He said to them : » Those who are well have no need of a medical man but those who are ill do . I have come to care for sinners not the upright . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have come' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); 'to care for sinners' is a purpose clause, not a direct object of 'come'.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' licenses an external argument (the speaker 'I' as agent) and introduces a purposive infinitival complement ('to care for sinners'), indicating an agentive, controlled action rather than an unaccusative change-of-state or motion event.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes change-of-location with the subject functioning as an internal argument (theme) rather than an external argument, and the infinitive phrase 'to care for sinners' functions as a purposive complement, not an internal argument of 'come' itself.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a punctual boundary transition (arrival at a location or state) without a durative process phase; it marks the instantaneous crossing of a threshold into a new condition, not a gradual unfolding toward a goal.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival at a purpose or destination), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials ('I have come at this moment'), and represents a boundary crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a bounded, punctual event with an inherent endpoint (arrival) and a defined result state (being present), not a dynamic durative situation without inherent completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have come' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not being present to being present for the purpose of caring for sinners), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a change-of-state event marking the onset of arrival with a purposive endpoint (to care for sinners), involving dynamic progression from one location/state to another, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have come to care for sinners' presents the situation as actually occurring in the actual world (Jesus stating his actual purpose), without any indication of counterfactuality, unfulfilled wishes, or conditional reasoning.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have come' is not negated; the negation 'not' in the sentence applies to the object 'the upright' (i.e., 'not the upright'), not to the eventuality of coming.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'come' describes a change of location where the mover (Jesus) has arrived at a destination and remains there at the reference time, with the resulting state (being present to care for sinners) currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival/purpose) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and there is no duration adverbial indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'come' is a transition event construed as a completed whole, and the clause presents a bounded occurrence (the arrival/coming itself) as a discrete assertion in the narrative.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have come' is asserted as a realized fact within Jesus's direct speech, stating his actual purpose in the world, not a projected, hypothetical, or desired situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have come' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'come' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have come' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'I' as the semantic agent (the one who came); it lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: When his own people heard of this they went to apprehend him . They said : » He has lost his senses . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has lost' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('He') and an overt direct object ('his senses'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns=1.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'lost' licenses an external argument (the subject 'he' as agent) and takes a direct object ('his senses'), making it transitive and not unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has lost' licenses an internal argument ('his senses' as direct object), therefore it does not instantiate ASUnrg which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'lose' in 'has lost his senses' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous change-of-state (transition into a state of having lost mental faculties) without a durative process phase, matching the achievement category.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has lost' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (loss of mental faculties), represents a boundary point of change rather than an unfolding process, and can be modified by specific time-point adverbials (e.g., 'has lost his senses at that moment'), meeting the criteria for AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has lost' denotes a bounded change of state with an inherent endpoint (transition from having senses to lacking them), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has lost his senses' entails a transition into a new resulting state (loss of mental faculties), which disqualifies it from being a semelfactive according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has lost' denotes a change-of-state event (loss of senses) involving dynamic progression from a prior state to a new state, which violates the stative requirement of lacking internal eventive unfolding.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'He has lost his senses' presents a situation understood to have actually occurred in the narrative world (his people's assessment of his current state), not a counterfactual condition contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negator 'not' does not appear in the sentence; 'has lost' is a positive assertion within reported speech, and no negative quantifiers, 'never', or 'neither...nor' constructions negate this gram.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has lost' describes a change of state (loss of mental faculties) where the resulting state (being senseless/irrational) is currently in effect at the reference time, as evidenced by the present context of their concern about apprehending him.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'has lost his senses' denotes a transition into a new state (loss of mental faculties) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for verbs entailing state transitions.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'lost' denotes a transition event (change of state from having senses to lacking them), and 'has lost' presents this transition as a completed whole bounded at a prior point, meeting the criterion for perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has lost' is embedded in reported speech ('They said: He has lost his senses') and asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world from the perspective of the speakers, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has lost' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'lost', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The form 'has lost' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of transitive verb 'lose'), with the grammatical subject 'He' as the semantic agent (the one who lost his senses), not the patient; passive voice requires 'have been' + past participle.

FullText: » If Satan has risen up against himself , he is divided , cannot stand and will come to an end .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has risen up' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); 'against himself' is a prepositional phrase modifying the verb, not a grammatical object.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'rise up' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument (Satan) undergoes displacement as an internal argument, with no external argument licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'risen up' denotes a change-of-location event where the sole argument (Satan) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing a change of state/position, not as an external argument initiating an activity.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has risen up' denotes a punctual boundary transition (movement from a lower to an upright position) without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, matching the achievement category.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has risen up' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (Satan moving from not-risen to risen), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials ('has risen up at dawn'), and represents a boundary crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has risen up' denotes a punctual, bounded event with an inherent endpoint (the completion of the rising action), not a dynamic durative situation; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without changing meaning, and represents a single completed occurrence rather than an ongoing activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has risen up' entails a transition into a new resulting state (Satan moving from a state of not being risen to a state of being risen), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has risen up' denotes a change-of-state event (Satan moving from a non-risen to risen position) involving dynamic progression and an internal eventive boundary, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'If Satan has risen up against himself' is an open conditional expressing a present possibility rather than a counterfactual condition, as indicated by the present perfect 'has risen' paired with present tense consequences ('is divided, cannot stand'), making it a factual conditional about possible current circumstances rather than a contrary-to-fact scenario.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has risen up' is not negated; the negation in the sentence ('cannot stand') applies to a different eventuality, not to the rising up itself.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'risen up' describes a change of location, but the conditional clause structure ('If Satan has risen up...') and the subsequent consequences ('he is divided, cannot stand') indicate a hypothetical scenario rather than a persisting resultant state at the reference time; the mover's current location is not established as held at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'has risen up' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (Satan moving from not-risen to risen) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and there is no duration adverbial indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has risen up' is a transition event (rise up = change of state/position) construed as a completed whole, presenting a bounded occurrence rather than an ongoing or stative situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has risen up' appears in a conditional construction ('If Satan has risen up...') that projects a hypothetical scenario rather than asserting a realized fact, triggering irrealis marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has risen up' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'risen', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'has risen up' is active voice with an intransitive verb; the subject 'Satan' is the semantic agent (the one doing the rising), not the patient, and there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle).

FullText: » These are they by the wayside where the word is sown . When they have heard ; Satan comes and takes away the word that has been sown in them .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have heard' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('they') and an overt direct object ('the word'), satisfying the transitive pattern required for ASTrns=1.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'hear' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'they' who perform the action of hearing), making it unergative rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have heard' licenses an internal argument (the word that is heard, realized as a relative clause 'that has been sown in them'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous perceptual transition into awareness without a durative process phase; it marks the boundary moment when the word enters perception, not a gradual change-of-state leading to a defined result.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (hearing the word), which is a near-instantaneous crossing of a definable threshold from not-hearing to hearing, matching the positive example 'They have heard the news.'
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness of the word, which is treated as a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have heard' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not having heard to having heard the word), which disqualifies it from being a semelfactive; additionally, the temporal clause structure implies a durative or completed state rather than a single punctual occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness, which encodes eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering the set_to_0_if criterion.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'When they have heard' presents a temporal sequence of actual events (hearing the word, then Satan taking it away) without any indication that the situation is contrary to fact; it describes a factual narrative scenario, not a counterfactual condition.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have heard' is not negated; the negation in the sentence ('takes away') applies to a different eventuality, not to the hearing itself.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have heard' describes a perception event without a resulting state that persists at the reference time; the clause merely reports a past interaction (hearing the word) that triggers Satan's subsequent action, not a state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'When they have heard' describes a discrete, bounded event (the act of hearing) that triggers a subsequent action (Satan comes and takes away), rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'When they have heard' presents the act of hearing as a bounded, completed transition event that serves as a discrete temporal anchor for the subsequent narrative action ('Satan comes and takes away'), functioning as a bounded step in the narrative sequence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have heard' appears in a temporal clause ('When they have heard') that asserts a realized condition triggering a subsequent action, not a projected or hypothetical situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have heard' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'heard' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have heard' is active voice (subject 'they' is the semantic agent performing the action of hearing), not passive voice; passive voice requires the form 'have been' + past participle, which is absent here.

FullText: » These are they by the wayside where the word is sown . When they have heard ; Satan comes and takes away the word that has been sown in them .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'has been sown' is morphologically passive and licenses both an external argument (implied agent) and an internal argument (the word), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause 'has been sown' is morphologically passive and the lexical verb 'sow' licenses an external argument (an implicit agent performing the sowing action), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for passive clauses.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'sown' is in passive voice with an overt internal argument ('the word'), making it a transitive construction that licenses an internal argument rather than an unergative one-participant event with an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The passive form 'has been sown' denotes a durative process of planting/scattering seeds that culminates in a defined result state (seeds embedded in soil), naturally compatible with 'in X time' modification (e.g., 'has been sown in an hour'), satisfying the accomplishment criterion of directed action toward a defined endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has been sown' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (seeds transitioning from unsown to sown), lacks measurable internal duration, and is construed as occurring at a definable temporal boundary rather than unfolding over time.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'sow' denotes a bounded action with an inherent endpoint (seeds are planted in the ground), and the passive perfect 'has been sown' marks a completed result state rather than an ongoing dynamic activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has been sown' entails a resulting change of state (seeds transition from not being sown to being sown in the ground), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has been sown' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect (present perfect progressive), which entails dynamic eventive unfolding and change-of-state (the action of sowing), making it incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'that has been sown in them' presents a situation that has actually occurred in the narrative world (the word was sown), without any indication of counterfactuality; it uses past perfect morphology to express a completed action within the narrative context, not a contrary-to-fact condition.

AktState_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (hearing the word), which encodes eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering the set_to_0_if criterion.

DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'When they have heard the word' describes a discrete, bounded event (the act of hearing) rather than a continuous situation extending to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual iteration markers required for DtUniv classification.

FullText: » When the grain is ready he immediately sends men to cut it , because the time for cutting has come . «

DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a transition into a new state (the arrival of the cutting time) rather than a situation holding continuously over a time span, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for verbs that entail transitions.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has come' is a transition event (arrival/occurrence of a time point) construed as a completed bounded whole, presenting the situation as a single discrete occurrence rather than ongoing or habitual.

FullText: Their keepers ran off to the town to tell others what happened . People came to see what had taken place .

AktState_explanation: The verb 'take place' denotes a bounded dynamic event (something happening/occurring) with an inherent endpoint, which involves eventive unfolding rather than a static condition holding over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'what had taken place' presents a situation that actually occurred in the narrative world (people came to see an event that did happen), using past perfect for temporal anteriority within the narrative, not expressing a counterfactual condition.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had taken place' is not negated; it appears in a positive relative clause describing what people came to see, with no negator directly modifying or negating the eventuality.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'take place' is an event verb describing an occurrence without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; the clause reports what happened in the past without indicating that a changed state currently holds.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'what had taken place' describes a discrete bounded event (something that happened) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and the verb 'take place' entails a punctual occurrence.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'take place' is a transition event denoting an occurrence, and 'had taken place' presents this as a single bounded, completed event in the narrative sequence, functioning as a discrete step in the discourse.

FullText: When they came to Jesus they saw the man who had been demon possessed . He was fully clothed and in full use of his senses . So they were afraid .

FullText: go into the village that is nearby . » As soon as you enter it you will find a colt tied there . No man has ever sat on that colt . Untie him and bring him .

DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'No man has ever sat on that colt' describes a discrete bounded event (the act of sitting) that has not occurred, rather than a continuous situation holding over time; the adverbial 'ever' emphasizes the absence of any such occurrence, not a duration.

FullText: When he taught , he said : » Is it not written , my house shall be called a house of prayer for all the nations ? But you have made it a den of robbers . «

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'made' licenses an external argument (agent 'you') and introduces a direct object 'it', satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria for verbs that license external arguments and contain overt grammatical objects.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'made' licenses an internal argument ('it') and a predicative complement ('a den of robbers'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have made' denotes a punctual change-of-state (transforming the house into a den of robbers) that occurs as a single boundary transition without an inherent durative process phase, fitting the achievement category rather than accomplishment.

DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'have made it a den of robbers' denotes a transition into a new state (changing the house from its intended purpose to a den of robbers) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for verbs entailing state transitions.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'you have made it a den of robbers' presents a completed transition event (the action of making/transforming the house into a den of robbers) as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint, fitting the perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have made' asserts a realized action in the actual world as a factual accusation, not as projected, hypothetical, or desired; it is a negative fact stated without modal, conditional, or future marking.

FullText: » Have you not read this Scripture : ‘ The stone the builders rejected was made the chief corner stone . ’

FullText: » The tribulation in these days will be greater then there has ever been from the beginning of God’s creation until now , and never shall be .
ASTrns_explanation: The target 'has ever been [tribulation]' functions as a copula construction linking the subject to a predicative complement, not introducing an internal argument; it expresses existence/state rather than a transitive event with an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The copular verb 'be' does not license an external argument; the sole argument 'tribulation' functions as an internal argument (theme) linked to a predicative complement, matching the copular unaccusative pattern.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'been' is a copula that does not license an internal argument; rather, 'tribulation' functions as the internal argument (subject complement/theme) in an existential/appearance construction ('there has ever been [tribulation]'), making the sole argument function as an internal argument rather than an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has been' denotes a punctual or stative condition (existence of tribulation) without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it expresses a boundary transition into a state rather than an accomplishment with inherent temporal development toward completion.
AktAch_explanation: The construction 'has ever been [tribulation]' denotes a stative condition of existence rather than a punctual transition into a new state; it describes an ongoing state of being without a definable threshold boundary.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'has (ever) been [tribulation]' denotes a bounded perceptual/existential state marking the onset or completion of a condition (tribulation existing), not a dynamic durative activity requiring sustained engagement; it lacks the homogeneous, ongoing character of activities like 'run' or 'swim'.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'has (ever) been [tribulation]' expresses a state of existence rather than a punctual dynamic event; it entails a resulting state condition (the state of being/existing) and does not constitute a single bounded occurrence without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has ever been' is morphologically marked with present perfect progressive aspect ('has been'), which entails dynamic unfolding and eventive change incompatible with stative readings; additionally, 'tribulation' denotes a dynamic, bounded event involving suffering and progression rather than a static condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'there has ever been [tribulation]' presents a factual statement about what has actually occurred in the past (from creation until now), without any counterfactual marking or indication that the situation is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has ever been' is modified by the adverb 'ever' in a negative polarity context (comparative construction 'greater than there has ever been'), and the clause contains 'never shall be' which establishes the negative polarity frame for the entire comparative structure.
DtRes_explanation: The copular construction 'has ever been [tribulation]' describes a stative condition rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state; it reports a past state without a persisting change of state at the reference time, consistent with the edge case rule for stative copulas.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'there has ever been [tribulation]' with the duration adverbial 'from the beginning of God's creation until now' denotes a state (existence of tribulation) that has held continuously across the entire time span from creation to the reference time, matching the DtUniv feature.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'there has ever been [tribulation]' is a stative predicate ('be') denoting an unbounded existential state without a construed boundary or transition, and the adverbial 'ever' extends the temporal scope rather than bounding it.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has ever been [tribulation]' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (that tribulation has existed from creation until now), despite appearing in a comparative construction; it is not marked as modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has ever been [tribulation]' contains 'been' followed by an implicit progressive or stative participle context, and more critically, it includes 'been' which indicates a progressive perfect form (present perfect progressive), matching the set_to_1_if criterion for progressive marking.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the noun 'tribulation' (a non-verbal predicate), making 'been' a lexical copula rather than a passive auxiliary; this is a copular construction equivalent to 'has ever existed', not a passive voice construction.

DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete completed action of telling (a punctual event with a result state) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have told' presents a completed communicative act (a transition from not-having-informed to having-informed) as a bounded whole, with the endpoint construed as achieved at the reference time.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have told' is a present perfect asserting a realized action in the actual world ('I have told you all things beforehand'), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.

FullText: » This ointment might have been sold for more than three hundred denarii . And the money could have been given to the poor . They scolded her . «

ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive (been sold), and the lexical verb 'sell' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent who would sell the ointment), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for passive clauses.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target verb 'might have been sold' is in passive voice where the grammatical subject 'ointment' functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing a change of state, not as an external argument (agent/initiator), thus failing the ASUnrg criterion.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'sold' denotes a transaction directed toward a defined goal (transfer of goods for money) with a natural completion point, and can be modified by 'in X time' (e.g., 'sold in an hour'), satisfying the accomplishment criteria despite the modal and passive framing.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'sold' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (change of ownership/possession) that can be modified by specific time-point adverbials and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying the achievement criteria despite the modal and perfect aspect markers.

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'sold' entails a transition into a resulting state (change of ownership/possession), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status regardless of the modal construction.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'sold' denotes a dynamic change-of-state event (transfer of ownership) involving internal eventive unfolding, and the perfect aspect 'have been sold' marks a bounded event with a clear endpoint, incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'might have been sold' occurs in an apodosis-like clause expressing an unfulfilled possibility contrary to fact, where the ointment was not actually sold for that price, as evidenced by the contrastive context ('They scolded her') indicating the event did not occur.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'might have been sold' contains no negator (not, n't, never, or negative quantifier) that negates the eventuality; the sentence expresses a counterfactual possibility, not negation of the event itself.
DtRes_explanation: The modal 'might have been' expresses counterfactual possibility rather than an actual change of state; no transition to a resulting state is entailed in reality at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (a hypothetical sale) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and contains no duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause uses 'might have been sold' (modal + perfect passive), which expresses a hypothetical/counterfactual possibility rather than asserting a bounded completed event as actually occurring; the modal 'might' introduces uncertainty and unreality that prevents the construal of a single bounded occurrence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram contains the modal auxiliary 'might' combined with 'have been sold', presenting a hypothetical, speculative situation that is not asserted as realized in the actual world.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'might have been sold' includes a modal auxiliary ('might') preceding 'have', which satisfies the set_to_1_if condition for modal perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'might have been sold' instantiates passive voice morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle 'sold' of transitive verb), with the grammatical subject 'ointment' functioning as the semantic patient (the entity being sold), satisfying all criteria for VcPass.

FullText: » This ointment might have been sold for more than three hundred denarii . And the money could have been given to the poor . They scolded her . «

FullText: » Truly I tell you everywhere the good news is preached through out the whole world , that which this woman has done will be spoken of for a memorial of her . «

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has done' describes a completed action (the woman's deed) whose resulting state—that the deed exists and will be commemorated—persists at the reference time, as indicated by the clause 'will be spoken of for a memorial of her.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'this woman has done' describes a discrete bounded event (a specific action performed by the woman) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and there are no duration adverbials indicating continuous extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has done' construes the woman's action as a single bounded, completed event (a transition from not-done to done) that will be memorialized, presenting it as a discrete accomplishment rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
