FullText: » The Son of man goes even as it is written of him . Woe to that man through whom the Son of man is betrayed ! It would have been better if he had not been born . «
ASTrns_explanation: The target 'would have been better' functions as a copula construction linking the subject to a predicative complement (better), not introducing an internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The copular verb 'be' in 'would have been better' does not license an external argument; the sole argument 'it' (referring to the condition) functions as an internal argument linked to the predicative complement 'better', matching the copular unaccusative pattern.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target 'would have been better' is a copular construction with 'better' as a predicative adjective describing a state; it does not denote an action with an external argument agent/initiator, and the sole argument functions as an internal argument (theme) rather than an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'would have been better' expresses a punctual state or condition (a comparative evaluation) rather than a durative process culminating in a defined endpoint; it denotes a stative quality without inherent temporal unfolding or completion structure.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'would have been better' denotes a stative condition or quality comparison rather than a punctual transition into a new state; it lacks the dynamic change-of-state semantics required for AktAch classification.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'would have been better' denotes a stative result condition (a defined state of comparative superiority) rather than a dynamic, durative situation requiring sustained engagement, and lacks the homogeneity and temporal unfolding characteristic of activities.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'would have been better' is a stative predicate expressing a resulting state or condition, not a dynamic punctual event, and therefore does not instantiate the semelfactive feature.
AktState_explanation: The target 'would have been better' denotes a static evaluative/relational state (a comparative quality condition) that holds without dynamic change, internal progression, or eventive unfolding, fitting the stative feature AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'would have been better' occurs in the apodosis of a counterfactual conditional ('if he had not been born'), expressing an unfulfilled contrary-to-fact situation where the event (the person being born) is understood to have actually occurred despite the wish that it had not.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'would have been better' is not negated; the negation 'not' in the sentence applies to the embedded clause 'if he had not been born', not to the target gram itself.
DtRes_explanation: The target is a stative copula construction ('would have been better') that describes a hypothetical condition rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state that holds at the reference time; it expresses a counterfactual comparison without a persisting change of state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause expresses a counterfactual conditional about a state transition (not being born), focusing on the resulting state rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual series indicators.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'better' is a stative adjective denoting a state without a construed boundary or transition, and the counterfactual conditional structure ('would have been') does not present a bounded occurrence but rather a hypothetical state contrary to fact.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'would have been better' contains the modal auxiliary 'would' combined with the perfect infinitive 'have been', presenting a counterfactual/hypothetical evaluation of an unrealized situation rather than asserting a realized fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'would have been better' includes the modal auxiliary 'would' preceding 'have', which satisfies the set_to_1_if condition for modal perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the adjective 'better' (a non-verbal predicate), not a past participle functioning as passive; 'been' functions as a lexical copula here, not a passive auxiliary.

FullText: » The Son of man goes even as it is written of him . Woe to that man through whom the Son of man is betrayed ! It would have been better if he had not been born . «
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('had not been born'), and the lexical verb 'born' licenses both an external argument (the agent/causer, unexpressed) and an internal argument (the patient 'he'), satisfying the passive condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The target 'had not been born' is a copular 'be' clause where the sole argument 'he' functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing a change-of-state event (coming into existence), and no external argument is licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'born' denotes a change-of-state/coming-into-being event where the sole argument (he) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) rather than an external argument, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for unaccusative verbs.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'had not been born' denotes a punctual boundary transition (the moment of coming into existence) without a durative process phase, matching the achievement category.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'be born' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (existence) that occurs at a definable temporal boundary, lacks measurable internal duration, and cannot be interrupted as an ongoing process phase.
AktAct_explanation: The situation denotes a defined result state (non-existence/not coming into being) rather than a dynamic durative activity; birth is a bounded punctual event with an inherent endpoint, not an ongoing action that unfolds over time.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'be born' entails a transition into a new resulting state (existence/life) and is not construed as a punctual occurrence without consequent result, thus it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had not been born' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect ('had been') combined with a change-of-state verb ('born'), denoting a bounded eventive transition from non-existence to existence, which involves dynamic change and an inherent endpoint incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'had not been born' occurs in the protasis of a counterfactual conditional ('if he had not been born'), expressing an unfulfilled contrary-to-fact condition paired with the apodosis 'It would have been better', which uses a modal perfect to indicate what would have resulted under different circumstances.
ClNegPol_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is negated with 'not' in 'had not been born', directly negating the eventuality of being born according to set_to_1_if criterion 1.
DtRes_explanation: The stative copula 'had not been born' describes a state rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state; it negates the occurrence of the birth event itself, so no change of state persists at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a counterfactual state (non-birth) as a transition into an alternative condition rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual markers indicating DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'he had not been born' is negated with 'not' in a counterfactual conditional context ('It would have been better if...'), indicating the transition has not occurred and the situation remains open/unrealized, which triggers the set_to_0 rule for negated transitions.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had not been born' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('If he had not been born'), presenting a hypothetical, non-actual situation contrary to fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had not been born' contains the morphologically past auxiliary 'had' followed by 'been' and a past participle, instantiating both past perfect and progressive perfect features.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had not been born' instantiates VcPass: it has the form auxiliary 'had' + 'been' + past participle 'born' of a transitive verb, the grammatical subject 'he' corresponds to the semantic patient (the entity brought into existence), and overt passive morphology is present.

FullText: He took a cup , and when he had given praise , he gave it to them and they all had a drink from it .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'give' introduces an overt grammatical subject (he) and an overt direct object (praise), satisfying the transitive pattern required for ASTrns=1.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'give' licenses an external argument (the subject 'he' as agent) and introduces an internal argument ('praise' as the direct object), thus failing the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'give' licenses an internal argument (the direct object 'praise'), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'had given praise' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous act of utterance/expression without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it is a boundary transition into the state of having praised, not a gradual change-of-state or directed process toward completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had given praise' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the completion of giving praise), can be modified by a specific time-point adverbial ('when he had given praise' marks a definable temporal boundary), and does not permit interruption as an ongoing process phase.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had given praise' denotes a single, punctual completed action with an inherent endpoint (the act of giving praise is finished), not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds over time without an endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had given praise' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not having given praise to having given it), which disqualifies it from being a semelfactive; additionally, the temporal clause structure construes it as a completed action with consequent results rather than a single punctual occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'give praise' denotes a bounded dynamic event with a clear endpoint and internal eventive unfolding, not a static condition that holds over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'when he had given praise' uses past perfect to express temporal anteriority within a narrative sequence (the praise-giving occurred before the giving of the cup), not to indicate a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had given praise' is not negated; no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never') or negative quantifier modifies it, and the clause presents the event as occurring within the narrative sequence.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'give praise' describes a communication/action event that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; it reports a past interaction without a currently binding or persisting state, similar to the negative example 'He has given me advice before.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'when he had given praise' describes a discrete, bounded event (the act of giving praise) that serves as a temporal marker for another event, not a situation holding continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'when he had given praise' presents a bounded transition event (giving praise) as a completed whole that serves as a discrete step in the narrative sequence ('when...he gave it to them'), construing the situation as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had given praise' is a past perfect form asserting a realized action within a temporal sequence of actual events (he took a cup, gave praise, gave it to them, they drank), with no modal, conditional, counterfactual, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the first condition for set_to_1_if.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had given praise' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'he' as the semantic agent (doer), not passive voice; it lacks the required auxiliary sequence 'have/had' + 'been' + past participle.

FullText: He came a third time and said to them : » Sleep and take your rest . It is enough the hour has come . Look , the Son of man is betrayed into the hands of sinners !
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has come' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object), matching the set_to_0_if criterion for verbs without overt internal arguments.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument (the hour) undergoes a transition into existence/arrival, with no external argument licensed, matching the unaccusative pattern.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a change-of-location or arrival event where the sole argument functions as an internal argument (theme) rather than an external argument, matching the unaccusative pattern exemplified by 'fall' in the negative examples.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual boundary transition into a state (arrival at a defined moment in time) without a durative process phase, matching the achievement category exemplified by 'arrive'.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival at a temporal boundary), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'the hour has come'), and represents an instantaneous crossing of a threshold rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual event with an inherent endpoint (arrival at a destination/moment), marking a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'the hour has come' entails a transition into a new resulting state (the hour arriving/beginning), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of awareness or completion of a change-of-state (the arrival of 'the hour'), which involves dynamic change and eventive unfolding rather than a static condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'the hour has come' presents a situation as actually occurring in the narrative world without any counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the event did not happen.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has come' is not negated; it appears in a positive assertion ('the hour has come') with no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never') or negative quantifier modifying it.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has come' is a motion verb describing arrival, but the context indicates the hour has arrived and passed (the betrayal is now happening), not that it remains at a destination; this is an experiential/perfective use rather than a resultative state persisting at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'has come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the arrival of the hour) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the pattern of discrete bounded events.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has come' is a transition event (arrival/occurrence) construed as a completed bounded whole, asserting that the hour has arrived as a single accomplished fact.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has come' asserts the hour as a realized fact in the actual world within the narrative context, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish markers present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has come' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'come', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has come' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of intransitive verb 'come'), not passive voice; passive voice requires 'have' + 'been' + past participle of a transitive verb.

FullText: We have heard his words against God . What is your opinion ? They all said he should be put to death .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have heard' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('We') and an overt direct object ('his words against God'), satisfying the transitive requirement.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an external argument (the subject 'we' as the agent/experiencer) and introduces a direct object ('his words'), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the clause contains an overt direct object introduced by the target verb.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an internal argument ('his words against God'), which is a direct object, so it does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'heard' denotes a punctual perceptual event—a near-instantaneous transition into awareness—without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, matching the achievement profile rather than accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (hearing/receiving the words), which is a near-instantaneous crossing of a cognitive threshold, matching the positive example 'They have heard the news.'
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness of 'his words against God', which is treated as a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have heard' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not having heard to having heard) and does not constitute a single punctual occurrence without consequent result, thus it is not semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (hearing/receiving his words), which is eventive change rather than a static condition, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for bounded perceptual events.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'We have heard his words against God' presents a situation that has actually occurred in the actual world (they did hear the words), with no indication of counterfactuality, unfulfilled wishes, or conditional reasoning.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have heard' is not negated; there is no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never') or negative quantifier modifying it, and the sentence presents the event as realized rather than absent.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'hear' is a perception event that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; the clause merely reports a past interaction without a persisting condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'We have heard his words against God' describes a discrete, bounded event of perceiving/receiving words rather than a continuous situation extending to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual markers required for DtUniv classification.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'hear' denotes a stative situation (perception/knowledge state) without a construed boundary or transition, and the clause does not present a bounded occurrence but rather an ongoing state of having knowledge.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have heard' is in the present perfect tense asserting a realized fact in the actual world (the speakers have actually heard his words), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context applying.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have heard' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'heard' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have heard' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'We' as the semantic agent (doer) and 'his words' as the grammatical object (semantic patient); it lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: Pilate said : » Why , what evil has he done ? « But they cried out exceedingly , » Impale him ! «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has done' introduces an overt internal argument 'evil' (direct object), satisfying the criterion for a transitive construction with both external argument (he) and internal argument (evil).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an external argument (the subject 'he'), and the clause contains a direct object 'evil', indicating an agentive transitive verb that does not meet unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has done' licenses an internal argument (the direct object 'evil'), therefore it does not instantiate ASUnrg which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'do' in 'has done evil' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous action without an inherent durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it lacks the gradual unfolding or measurable duration characteristic of accomplishments.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has done (evil)' denotes a durative accomplishment—committing evil acts unfolds over measurable duration rather than occurring at a single punctual boundary transition, and it can naturally be modified by 'finish' (e.g., 'finish doing evil'), which indicates a completed durative process phase.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'has done evil' denotes a bounded, completed action with an inherent endpoint (the evil act itself), not a dynamic durative situation; it cannot naturally be modified by 'for X time' without changing meaning, and it lacks the homogeneous property required for activity classification.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has done (evil)' entails a resulting change of state or condition (the commission of wrongdoing with lasting consequences), not a single punctual occurrence without result, and the perfect aspect construes it as a durative situation with a consequent state rather than a semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has done' denotes a bounded dynamic event (committing evil acts) involving change and eventive unfolding, which is incompatible with the stative feature AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'what evil has he done' is an embedded interrogative introduced by 'what' (indirect question), presenting a factual inquiry about whether an evil act occurred in the actual world, not a counterfactual condition contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negator 'what' functions as an interrogative pronoun in a question, not as a negator of the eventuality; the clause 'has he done' is not negated but rather questioned for specification of the evil committed.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has done' reports a past action (committing evil) without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; it is a bare activity verb that does not encode a transition to a new state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'what evil has he done' describes a discrete bounded event (a specific wrongdoing) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual markers indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'what evil has he done' presents a completed action as a bounded whole without internal temporal development, construing the doing of evil as a single accomplished event rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has done' appears in an interrogative clause ('What evil has he done?') that seeks confirmation of an actual fact or state of affairs in the real world, not a projected or hypothetical situation, thus asserting realis rather than irrealis.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has done' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'done', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'has done' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'he' as the semantic agent (doer) and the object 'evil' as the semantic patient; it lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: At the ninth hour Jesus cried with a loud voice , saying : » Eloi , Eloi , lama sabachthani ? « Interpreted it means , » My God , my God , why have you forsaken me ? «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('you') and an overt direct object ('me'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns marking.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'forsaken' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'you'/God) that acts upon the internal argument 'me', making it a transitive verb with an agent, not an unaccusative verb.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'forsaken' licenses an internal argument ('me'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'forsaken' denotes a punctual state-change (transition into abandonment) without a durative process phase, and the perfect aspect marks a completed boundary transition rather than a measured duration toward a goal.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (abandonment/desertion) that can be modified by specific time-point adverbials and does not permit interruption as an ongoing process, satisfying the criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' denotes a completed action with a defined result state (abandonment), naturally modified by 'in X time' rather than 'for X time', and lacks the homogeneous, durative quality required for AktAct classification.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have forsaken' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not being forsaken to being forsaken) and is not construed as a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' denotes a change-of-state (abandonment/separation) involving dynamic progression from a prior state of presence to absence, which is a bounded eventive situation incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'why have you forsaken me' is a direct utterance expressing a present emotional state and question about an actual situation (Jesus's perceived abandonment), not a counterfactual condition contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have forsaken' is not negated; it appears in a question ('why have you forsaken me?') where the negation is semantic/pragmatic rather than syntactic, and no negator (not, n't, never, no, nothing) directly negates the auxiliary or the eventuality itself.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'forsaken' (past participle of 'forsake') describes a change of state—abandonment or withdrawal of support—and the clause 'why have you forsaken me?' indicates that this resulting state of abandonment currently holds at the reference time of Jesus's utterance.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' denotes a transition into a new state (abandonment) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and the focus is on that transition rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'forsaken' is a transition event (abandonment/departure from a state) construed as a completed whole, and the present perfect 'have forsaken' presents this transition as a bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint rather than as ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have forsaken' appears in a direct quotation of Jesus's utterance, asserting a realized state of abandonment as an actual fact within the narrative world, not as a projected, hypothetical, or desired situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have forsaken' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have you forsaken' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'you' as the semantic agent (the one doing the forsaking) and the semantic patient 'me' as the grammatical object; it lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: He bought a linen cloth , and taking him down , wound him in the linen cloth . Then he laid him in a tomb that had been cut out of a rock . He rolled a stone against the door of the tomb .
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('had been cut') and the lexical verb 'cut' licenses both an external argument (agent, unexpressed) and an internal argument (patient: 'a tomb'), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('had been cut'), and the lexical verb 'cut' licenses an external argument (an agent who performed the cutting), even though it is suppressed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'cut' licenses an internal argument (the tomb as the theme/patient undergoing the change-of-state of being carved), and in the passive construction 'had been cut', the sole overt argument functions as an internal argument rather than an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'cut' denotes a gradual change-of-state (carving/excavating rock) that culminates in a defined result state (a tomb that is cut out), satisfying the durative process directed toward a defined goal criterion.
AktAch_explanation: The past perfect 'had been cut' describes a completed durative process of carving/cutting a tomb from rock, which unfolds over measurable duration rather than occurring at a punctual boundary transition.
AktAct_explanation: The passive construction 'had been cut out of a rock' denotes a completed action with an inherent endpoint (a tomb fully carved from rock), not a dynamic durative activity without a defined goal.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had been cut' entails a resulting change of state (a tomb carved from rock), which disqualifies it from being semelfactive according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had been cut' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect (had been + -en participle), which entails dynamic eventive unfolding and change-of-state, incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'that had been cut out of a rock' uses past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (the tomb's cutting preceded the laying of the body), describing a factual situation that occurred in the actual world, with no indication of counterfactuality.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been cut' is not negated; it describes a realized state (the tomb was cut out of rock), and no negator applies to this eventuality.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'had been cut out of a rock' is a stative copular construction describing the state of the tomb rather than encoding a transition; it reports a pre-existing condition without entailing that a change of state is currently in effect at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'that had been cut out of a rock' describes a completed action resulting in a state (the tomb exists as cut), not a situation holding continuously from a past point to the reference time; it focuses on the transition/result rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The passive form 'had been cut' presents the tomb-carving as a bounded, completed transition event (the cutting out of the rock), with the endpoint construed as prior to the main narrative action, fitting the criterion for perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been cut' asserts a realized past state of affairs (the tomb's prior construction) as a fact within the narrative, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had been cut' contains the morphologically past auxiliary 'had' followed by 'been' and a progressive participle, satisfying two conditions for TLTS=1: past auxiliary marking and progressive perfect structure.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been cut' instantiates VcPass: it has the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of transitive verb 'cut', the grammatical subject 'tomb' is the semantic patient (the entity affected by the cutting event), and the agent is omitted.

FullText: They went to the tomb when the sun had risen . It was very early .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had risen' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it describes a state/event with only an external argument (the sun) and no affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'rise' denotes directed motion/change-of-location (the sun undergoes displacement upward) where the sole argument functions as an internal argument (theme) and no external argument is licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes a change-of-location event where the sole argument (the sun) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing upward motion, not as an external argument initiating an action.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'rise' denotes a punctual boundary transition (the sun crossing the horizon) without a durative process phase; it marks an instantaneous change-of-state from below to above the horizon, not a gradual culmination toward a defined goal.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had risen' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the sun crossing the horizon), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'at dawn'), and represents a definable threshold crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had risen' denotes a specific, inherent endpoint (the sun reaching a risen state) and marks a completed change of state, not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had risen' entails a transition into a resulting state (the sun being up), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had risen' denotes a change-of-state event (the sun moving from below to above the horizon) with a clear boundary marking the onset of a new condition, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'when the sun had risen' uses past perfect to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (the sun's rising occurred before they went to the tomb), describing an event that actually occurred in the story world, with no indication of counterfactuality.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had risen' is not negated; there is no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never') or negative quantifier modifying it, and the sentence presents the rising of the sun as an actual event.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had risen' describes a motion/change of state where the sun has moved to a position above the horizon and remains there at the reference time (when they went to the tomb), satisfying the resultative condition for motion verbs.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete, bounded event (the sun rising to completion) rather than a situation holding continuously over a time span; the focus is on the transition into a new state (sunrise) that occurred before the main event.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'had risen' is a transition event (the sun moving from below to above the horizon) construed as a completed whole at the reference time, presenting a bounded occurrence rather than an ongoing or stative situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had risen' is a past perfect form asserting a realized event in the actual world (the sun had already risen when they went to the tomb); it is not presented as projected, hypothetical, or non-actual, and no modal, conditional, or irrealis context applies.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the set_to_1_if condition for past perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had risen' is an active voice construction (auxiliary 'had' + past participle of intransitive verb 'rise'); the grammatical subject 'the sun' is the semantic agent/experiencer, not the patient, and there is no passive morphology ('have' + 'been' + past participle).

FullText: He said to them : » Do not be amazed . Jesus the Nazarene , who has been impaled , is not here . He has risen . Behold , the place where they laid him !
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been impaled'), and the lexical verb 'impale' licenses both an external argument (agent—those who impaled him) and an internal argument (patient—'Jesus the Nazarene'), satisfying the passive condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been impaled'), and the lexical verb 'impale' licenses an external argument (an agent who performed the impaling), even though it is not overtly expressed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'impale' is transitive and licenses an internal argument (the patient being impaled); in the passive construction 'has been impaled', the subject 'Jesus' functions as the internal argument (theme/patient) rather than an external argument (agent/initiator).
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'impale' denotes a punctual, near-instantaneous change-of-state (transition from unimpaled to impaled state) without a durative process phase, matching the achievement category.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has been impaled' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (the crossing of the threshold from alive/unharmed to pierced/dead), which is a definable boundary event that does not unfold over measurable duration and naturally permits time-point modification (e.g., 'was impaled at noon').
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has been impaled' denotes a bounded event with an inherent endpoint and defined result state (the state of being impaled), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has been impaled' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not-impaled to impaled condition) and is presented in perfect aspect indicating a persistent result state, not a single punctual occurrence without consequences.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has been impaled' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect and denotes a bounded change-of-state event (the action of impaling), which involves dynamic eventive unfolding incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'who has been impaled' presents a situation that actually occurred in the actual world (Jesus was crucified/impaled), without any indication of counterfactuality; it is a factual past event embedded in a narrative statement.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'is not here' applies to the location/presence of Jesus, not to the eventuality 'has been impaled'; the target gram itself is not negated.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'who has been impaled' uses a stative copular construction ('has been') that describes a resulting condition rather than encoding a transition; the impalement itself occurred in the past, and the clause reports a state without indicating that the impaled state currently persists at the reference time (the context indicates Jesus has risen and is no longer there).
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'has been impaled' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from alive to impaled) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The passive form 'has been impaled' presents a bounded transition event (impalement) as a completed whole, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for passive transitions that assert bounded events rather than ongoing processes.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has been impaled' asserts a realized fact in the actual world (Jesus was crucified), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has been impaled' includes 'been' followed by a progressive participle ('impaled'), which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for progressive perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has been impaled' instantiates VcPass: it has the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of transitive verb 'impale', the grammatical subject 'Jesus the Nazarene' is the semantic patient (the one affected by impalement), and overt passive morphology is present.

FullText: He said to them : » Do not be amazed . Jesus the Nazarene , who has been impaled , is not here . He has risen . Behold , the place where they laid him !
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has risen' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object), matching the set_to_0_if criterion for verbs without overt internal arguments.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument (He) undergoes displacement as an internal argument, with no external argument licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes a change-of-location event in which the sole argument (Jesus) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing a change of state/location rather than as an external argument initiating an action.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a punctual boundary transition from a state of being dead/laid in the tomb to a state of being alive/risen, without a durative process phase inherent to the event itself.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from being dead/laid in the tomb to being alive/risen), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'has risen at dawn'), and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying the criteria for AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a change of state with an inherent endpoint (transition from a lower to a higher position or from death to life), entailing a specific result state rather than a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has risen' entails a transition into a new resulting state (from being dead/laid in the tomb to being alive/risen), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a change-of-state event (from a state of being dead/impaled to a state of being alive/risen) involving dynamic progression and an event boundary, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'He has risen' presents a situation understood to have actually occurred in the narrative world, not a counterfactual condition contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has risen' is not negated; the negation in the sentence ('is not here') applies to a different clause and does not negate the eventuality of rising.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has risen' describes a change of state (from dead/impaled to alive/risen) and the context indicates the resulting state persists at the reference time, as evidenced by the parallel structure with 'is not here' and the assertion that the place is now empty.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from being dead/impaled to being alive/risen) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the negative example pattern of transition-focused verbs.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has risen' is a transition event (from dead to alive) construed as a completed whole, presenting the situation as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint, fitting the perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has risen' asserts a realized fact in the actual world within the speaker's declaration, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has risen' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'risen', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has risen' lacks the passive morphology required (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); 'risen' is the past participle of an intransitive verb, and the grammatical subject 'He' functions as the semantic agent/undergoer of the change-of-state, not as a patient of a transitive verb in passive voice.

FullText: They fled from the tomb , for trembling and astonishment had come on them . They said nothing to anyone for they were afraid .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'come on' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('trembling and astonishment') and an overt direct object ('them'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns marking.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come on' denotes a change-of-state/appearance event where the sole argument 'trembling and astonishment' functions as an internal argument (theme) undergoing a change of state (coming into being/appearing), with no external argument licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come on' licenses an internal argument ('trembling and astonishment'), making the subject 'them' function as an indirect object (recipient/experiencer) rather than an external argument/agent, thus failing the ASUnrg criteria.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'come on' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous change-of-state (the onset of trembling and astonishment) without a durative process phase, matching the achievement category.
AktAch_explanation: The verb phrase 'had come on' denotes a punctual transition into a new emotional/physical state (trembling and astonishment suddenly affecting them), which is a near-instantaneous change-of-state event that can be modified by specific time-point adverbials and does not permit interruption as an ongoing process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb phrase 'had come on' denotes a bounded perceptual/emotional event marking the onset of a state (trembling and astonishment befalling them), which is a single completed occurrence with an inherent endpoint, not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had come on them' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not having trembling/astonishment to having it), which disqualifies it from being semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'had come on' denotes a change-of-state event (the onset of trembling and astonishment), marking a bounded dynamic transition rather than a static condition that holds over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'trembling and astonishment had come on them' uses past perfect to express temporal anteriority within a narrative sequence (the trembling/astonishment occurred before they fled), presenting an actual event that happened in the world, not a counterfactual situation.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had come on' is not negated; the sentence affirms that trembling and astonishment had come upon them, and no negator (not, n't, never, no, nothing, neither) directly negates this eventuality.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'come on' describes a change of state (trembling and astonishment transitioning from absent to present), and the resulting state holds at the reference time, as evidenced by the consequent clause 'They said nothing to anyone for they were afraid,' which indicates the emotional state persists.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'had come on' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (trembling and astonishment arriving/occurring), not a situation holding continuously across a time span, matching the negative example pattern of transition-focused verbs.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'come on' is a transition event (onset of a state), and 'had come on them' presents this transition as a single bounded, completed occurrence that explains their emotional state at the reference time.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had come on' is a past perfect form asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world (trembling and astonishment did occur), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past tense, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'had come on' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); 'come' is a past participle used in active voice with the subject 'trembling and astonishment' as the semantic agent/experiencer, not the patient.

FullText: She told those who had been with him , for they mourned and wept .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had been with' introduces an overt internal argument ('him'), satisfying the requirement for a two-participant transitive construction with an external argument (those) and an internal argument (him).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'be with' licenses an external argument (the subject 'those' who actively maintained companionship with him), making it unergative rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had been with' licenses an internal argument ('him'), making it a transitive construction that does not meet the ASUnrg criteria of a one-participant event with no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The phrase 'had been with him' denotes a stative condition of accompaniment without an inherent endpoint or durative process directed toward completion; it lacks the bounded, goal-oriented structure required for AktAcc.
AktAch_explanation: The phrase 'had been with him' denotes a durative state of accompanying or being in someone's company over a measurable duration, not a punctual transition into a new result state.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'had been with him' denotes a dynamic, durative situation of sustained companionship/presence that unfolds over time without an inherent endpoint, satisfying the criteria for an activity (AktAct).
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'had been with' expresses a stative condition of accompaniment that unfolds over a measurable duration rather than a single punctual occurrence, and does not match the semelfactive profile of instantaneous dynamic events without resulting state change.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had been with' is morphologically marked as progressive aspect (past perfect progressive), which according to CRI set_to_0_if entails dynamic unfolding and is incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'who had been with him' uses past perfect to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (they were with him before she told them), describing an actual event that occurred, with no indication of counterfactuality.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been with' is not negated; the negator 'not' does not appear in the clause, and no negative quantifiers, 'never', or 'neither...nor' constructions negate this eventuality.
DtRes_explanation: The copular construction 'had been with him' describes a stative condition (being in someone's company) rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state; it does not entail a change of state that persists at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'had been with him' denotes a continuous state of companionship extending from a past point to the reference time (when she told them), fitting the pattern of a non-terminating situation holding throughout the time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'had been with' denotes a stative situation (being in someone's company) without a construed boundary or transition, and the clause does not present the situation as a bounded whole but rather as an ongoing state that overlaps with the narrative context.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been with' asserts a realized past state of affairs (those people had actually been with him); it is embedded in a relative clause providing factual description, not in a modal, conditional, counterfactual, or hypothetical context.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, and 'been' is followed by a prepositional phrase functioning as a progressive-like construction, satisfying the condition for past perfect with progressive marking.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the preposition 'with' (introducing a PP), not a past participle; 'been' functions as a copula here, not a passive auxiliary, so VcPass is not instantiated.

FullText: When they heard that he was alive and she had seen him they did not believe it .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had seen' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('she') and an overt direct object ('him'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns=1.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'had seen' licenses an external argument (the subject 'she' as agent/experiencer) and introduces a direct object ('him'), thus failing the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had seen' licenses an internal argument ('him'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'had seen' denotes a punctual perceptual event—a single instantaneous transition into visual awareness—without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, thus instantiating achievement rather than accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had seen' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (seeing/perceiving someone), which is a momentary crossing of a threshold from not-seeing to seeing, meeting the criterion for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (seeing him), which is a single completed occurrence without durative unfolding, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for bounded perceptual events.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had seen' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not having seen to having seen) and does not constitute a single punctual occurrence without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the completion of awareness (seeing him), which encodes eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering set_to_0_if criterion 4.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'she had seen him' presents a situation that actually occurred in the narrative world (she did see him), using past perfect for temporal anteriority within the reported event, not expressing a counterfactual condition.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'did not believe' applies to the matrix verb 'believe', not to the target gram 'had seen', which is embedded in a subordinate clause and remains unnegated.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had seen' describes a perception event (seeing) that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; it merely reports a past interaction without a persisting condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'she had seen him' describes a discrete bounded event (a single past occurrence of seeing) rather than a continuous situation extending to the reference time, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had seen' presents a bounded, completed transition event (the act of seeing/encountering him) as a discrete occurrence in the narrative sequence, functioning as a step in the reported events that led to their disbelief.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had seen' is in a reported speech context asserting a realized fact (she did see him), not a modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical construction.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had seen' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'she' as the semantic agent (the one doing the seeing) and 'him' as the grammatical object/semantic patient; it lacks the passive morphology 'have/had + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: Afterward he was revealed to the eleven as they sat at the table . He rebuked them for their unbelief and hardness of heart , because they did not believe those who had seen him after he had risen .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had seen' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('those who') and an overt direct object ('him'), satisfying the transitive pattern requirement for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'had seen' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'those who'), and the clause contains an overt direct object 'him', making it a transitive verb that does not meet the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had seen' licenses an internal argument ('him'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'had seen' denotes a punctual perceptual event (coming into visual awareness of the risen figure) that is construed as a single instantaneous transition into perception, lacking a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had seen' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (witnessing the risen figure), which is a definable threshold event that can be modified by specific time-point adverbials and does not permit interruption as an ongoing process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (seeing him after he had risen), which is a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation.
AktSemel_explanation: The pluperfect 'had seen' describes a state of knowledge resulting from a prior event (witnessing the risen Jesus), entailing a resulting change of state rather than a single punctual occurrence without consequences.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (witnessing the risen figure), which involves eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering the set_to_0_if criterion for bounded perceptual events.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'those who had seen him after he had risen' uses past perfect to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (the seeing occurred before the rebuking), not to indicate a counterfactual situation; the event actually occurred in the narrative world.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'did not believe' applies to the matrix verb 'believe', not to the target gram 'had seen', which appears in a relative clause describing those who witnessed the risen figure.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had seen' describes a past perception event without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; it merely reports that witnesses observed the risen figure, not that a change of state currently holds.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'those who had seen him after he had risen' describes a discrete bounded event (witnessing the risen Jesus) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the completed action of seeing rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'those who had seen him' presents a stative situation (the state of having witnessed) without a construed boundary or transition; it describes a persistent condition rather than a bounded completed event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had seen' asserts a realized past event within a relative clause describing those who witnessed the resurrection; it is not marked as modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical, and thus instantiates realis rather than irrealis.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had seen' is a past perfect form with morphologically past auxiliary 'had' followed by past participle 'seen', which instantiates TLTS according to the set_to_1_if rule for past auxiliary marking.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had seen' is active voice (subject 'those' is the semantic agent/doer), not passive voice; passive voice requires the auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle.

FullText: Afterward he was revealed to the eleven as they sat at the table . He rebuked them for their unbelief and hardness of heart , because they did not believe those who had seen him after he had risen .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had risen' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it describes a state or event without an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes a change-of-location/directed motion event where the sole argument (he) undergoes displacement and no external argument is licensed, matching the unaccusative pattern.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes a change-of-location event where the sole argument (he) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing the change, not as an external argument/agent, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for unaccusative verbs.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'had risen' denotes a punctual boundary transition (from dead to alive/risen state) without a durative process phase, matching the achievement category exemplified by 'arrive' and 'die'.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had risen' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from dead to alive/resurrected), lacks measurable internal duration, and represents a definable threshold crossing that can be modified by specific time-point adverbials.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had risen' denotes a completed change of state with an inherent endpoint (transition from dead to alive/risen), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without a defined result state.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had risen' entails a transition into a new resulting state (from dead to alive/resurrected), which disqualifies it from being semelfactive according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had risen' denotes a change-of-state event (resurrection/rising up) involving dynamic progression from one state to another, which is incompatible with the stative feature AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'after he had risen' uses past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (the resurrection is presented as an actual event that occurred), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'did not believe' applies to the matrix verb 'believe', not to the target gram 'had risen', which appears in a subordinate temporal clause describing when the seeing occurred.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'risen' describes a change of location (from dead/in tomb to risen/alive) where the mover (he/Jesus) has moved to a resulting state (risen) that persists at the reference time of the narrative.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'after he had risen' describes a discrete, bounded event (the resurrection itself) rather than a situation holding continuously over a time span, making it a punctual transition rather than a durative state.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'had risen' is a transition event (rise = change of state/position) construed as a completed whole in past time, presenting the resurrection as a single bounded occurrence rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had risen' is embedded in a relative clause ('after he had risen') that asserts a realized fact in the actual world—the resurrection is presented as a completed event that occurred, not as hypothetical, projected, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the set_to_1_if condition for past auxiliary marking.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had risen' is an intransitive verb in active voice (past perfect of 'rise'), not a passive construction; it lacks the required 'have + been + past participle' morphology and the subject 'he' is the semantic agent/undergoer performing the action, not a patient affected by an external agent.

FullText: After he had spoken to them the Lord Jesus , was received up into heaven , and sat down at the right hand of God .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had spoken' takes a prepositional phrase 'to them' rather than a direct object, and lacks an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object) that would satisfy the ASTrns feature requirements.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'spoken' licenses an external argument (the agent 'he') and introduces an internal argument ('to them'), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the verb licenses an external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'spoken' licenses an internal argument ('to them'), which is a prepositional indirect object, thus failing the requirement for ASUnrg that no internal argument be licensed or realized.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'speak' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous communicative act without an inherent durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it cannot naturally be modified by 'in X time' to indicate bounded completion of a gradual process.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had spoken' denotes a durative communicative event that unfolds over measurable duration rather than a punctual transition into a new state; speaking is an activity with internal temporal structure, not a boundary-crossing achievement.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had spoken' denotes a completed communicative act with an inherent endpoint (the completion of speaking to them), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without a defined goal.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had spoken' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not having spoken to having spoken) and is construed as a completed action with consequent conditions, not a single punctual occurrence without result.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had spoken' denotes a bounded dynamic event (speech act) with a clear endpoint and internal eventive unfolding, which fails the stative criteria and matches the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-state and dynamic activity.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'had spoken' uses past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within a narrative sequence (the speaking occurred before the receiving into heaven), without any indication that the situation is contrary to fact or unfulfilled.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had spoken' is not negated; the negator 'not' does not appear in the sentence, no negative quantifiers modify it, and 'never' does not modify it.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had spoken' describes a communication event that occurred prior to the main events (Jesus being received into heaven); it does not entail a resulting state that persists at the reference time, as the clause merely reports a past interaction without a persisting communicative state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (speaking to them) that serves as a temporal boundary for subsequent events, not a situation holding continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'had spoken' is a transition event (speech act with a construed endpoint) presented as a completed whole in a narrative sequence ('After he had spoken...'), functioning as a discrete bounded step that precedes subsequent events.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had spoken' is a past perfect form asserting a realized event in the actual world (he did speak to them), not a projected, hypothetical, or counterfactual situation; the temporal sequence 'After he had spoken...was received up' presents both as completed facts.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had spoken' is active voice (auxiliary 'had' + past participle of intransitive 'speak'), not passive voice; the grammatical subject 'he' is the semantic agent (the one doing the speaking), not the patient.

FullText: Many have attempted to write an orderly narrative about the things that have taken place .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'attempted' has no overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); the infinitival complement 'to write' is not a grammatical object of 'attempted' but rather part of a control structure, so the transitive feature is not instantiated.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'attempt' licenses an external argument (the agent 'many' who perform the action of attempting), and the infinitival complement 'to write' further indicates an agentive, volitional action, thus failing the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'attempted' licenses an internal argument (the infinitival complement 'to write'), making it a transitive construction that does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'attempt' denotes a punctual transition into the state of trying, lacking an inherent durative process phase directed toward a defined endpoint; it can be modified by 'for X time' (attempted for hours) without implying completion of a bounded goal.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'attempt' denotes an activity or effort that unfolds over measurable duration rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and it can naturally occur as the complement of 'finish' (e.g., 'finish attempting'), indicating a durative process phase.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'attempted' denotes a bounded event with an inherent endpoint (the attempt itself is a single, completed occurrence), and the infinitival complement 'to write an orderly narrative' specifies a defined goal, making this a telic accomplishment rather than an atelic activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have attempted' entails a transition into a resulting state (the attempt itself as a completed action with consequences) and unfolds over measurable duration rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, thus it is not semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'attempted' denotes a dynamic, bounded event involving volitional action and internal progression toward a goal, which entails eventive unfolding incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'Many have attempted to write an orderly narrative' presents a situation that has actually occurred in the actual world without any counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication of unfulfilled wishes.

FullText: Many have attempted to write an orderly narrative about the things that have taken place .

DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'things that have taken place' describes discrete bounded events (things that occurred) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have taken place' is a transition event (events occurring/happening) construed as a bounded whole, presenting the situations as completed occurrences rather than ongoing or habitual.

FullText: Most excellent Theophilus Greek : Theo philos : friend of God : I have accurately traced the course of all things from the beginning . Now it seems good to me to write this report to you .

TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have traced' is a present perfect asserting a completed action as a realized fact in the actual world, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.

FullText: » He will go before God in the spirit and power of Elijah . He will turn the hearts of fathers to their children and wrongdoers to the way of righteousness . This will make ready a people whose hearts have been directed to Jehovah . «

ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has looked on' is not negated; there is no negator ('not', 'n't', negative quantifier, or 'never') that applies to this verb phrase.

FullText: » He that is mighty has done great things to me . Holy is his name .
