AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a punctual boundary transition (from dead to alive/resurrected) without a durative process phase, matching the achievement category exemplified by 'arrive' and 'die'.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from dead to alive/resurrected), represents a boundary crossing into a definable result state, and can be modified by specific time-point adverbials (e.g., 'has risen at dawn'), meeting the criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a change of state with an inherent endpoint (transition from dead to alive/resurrected), entailing a defined result state rather than a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has risen' entails a transition into a new resulting state (from dead to alive/resurrected), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a change-of-state event (from dead to alive/resurrected) involving dynamic progression and an eventive boundary, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'one of the old prophets has risen again' presents a reported belief or possibility within indirect speech ('Others say...'), not a counterfactual condition; it describes what people claim may have occurred, without marking the situation as contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has risen' is not negated; the negation 'not' in 'decided not to go' pattern does not apply here, and no negative quantifiers, 'never', or 'neither...nor' constructions negate the eventuality of rising.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has risen' describes a motion/change of state where the subject (one of the old prophets) has moved from a prior state (dead) to a new state (alive/risen) that persists at the reference time, matching the resultative pattern of motion verbs with a destination state that holds currently.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a transition into a new state (from dead to alive/resurrected) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for verbs entailing transitions.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has risen' is a transition event (rising from death) construed as a completed whole, presenting the situation as a single bounded occurrence rather than ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has risen' is embedded in reported speech ('say one of the old prophets has risen again') and asserts a realized state of affairs within the reported belief, not a modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical construction.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has risen' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'risen', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has risen' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + intransitive past participle 'risen'), not passive voice; the grammatical subject 'one of the old prophets' is the semantic agent/undergoer of the action, and there is no 'been' auxiliary or 'by'-phrase required for passive marking.

FullText: When the voice came Jesus was found alone . They kept quiet and told no man what they had seen .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'had seen' introduces an overt internal argument ('what'), satisfying the requirement for a direct object in a transitive construction.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'had seen' licenses an external argument (the implicit subject 'they'), making it unergative/transitive rather than unaccusative; the sole argument functions as an external argument (agent/experiencer), not an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'seen' licenses an internal argument (the implied object of perception), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'had seen' denotes a punctual perceptual event (instantaneous transition into awareness) without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, fitting the achievement category rather than accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had seen' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (perceiving/witnessing an event), which is a boundary transition into a new cognitive state, matching the positive example 'They have heard the news.'
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (seeing something that happened), which is a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had seen' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not having witnessed something to having witnessed it) and does not qualify as a single punctual occurrence without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the completion of awareness (seeing something), which encodes eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering set_to_0_if criterion 4.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'what they had seen' presents a situation that actually occurred in the narrative world (they did see something), using past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority without any counterfactual marking.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'no' in 'told no man' negates the act of telling, not the target gram 'had seen'; the negation does not apply to the eventuality expressed by 'had seen'.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had seen' describes a perception event (seeing) that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; it merely reports a past interaction without a persisting condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'what they had seen' describes a discrete bounded event (the act of witnessing something) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual markers indicating continuous extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'what they had seen' presents the act of seeing as a completed, bounded event (a transition from not-seeing to seeing) that occurred prior to the narrative moment of keeping quiet and telling no one, functioning as a discrete bounded occurrence in the narrative sequence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had seen' asserts a realized past event (what the disciples actually witnessed) embedded in a narrative of facts, not a modal, conditional, counterfactual, or hypothetical construction.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had seen' is active voice (subject 'they' is the semantic agent performing the action of seeing), not passive voice; passive voice requires the form 'have/had + been + past participle' with the subject as semantic patient.

FullText: » Woe to you Chorazin ! Woe to you Bethsaida ! If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon , which were done in you , they would have repented long ago , sitting in sackcloth and ashes .
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive with the lexical verb 'done' licensing both an external argument (agent, unexpressed) and an internal argument (patient: 'mighty works'), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('had been done'), and the lexical verb 'do' licenses an external argument (an implicit agent performing the mighty works), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for passive clauses.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' (past participle in passive voice) licenses an internal argument ('mighty works' as the theme/patient), and the sole argument functions as an internal argument rather than an external argument, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for change-of-state events with internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: The passive construction 'had been done' denotes a punctual boundary transition into a completed state without describing a durative process phase; the event is construed as a single instantaneous accomplishment rather than an unfolding process directed toward a goal.
AktAch_explanation: The passive construction 'had been done' denotes a punctual transition into a completed state of performance (the mighty works coming into existence/occurrence), which is a change-of-state event that can be modified by specific time-point adverbials and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying the achievement criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The passive construction 'had been done' denotes a bounded, completed event with an inherent endpoint (the accomplishment of specific mighty works), not a dynamic durative activity without defined result state.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'mighty works had been done' entails a resulting change of state (miracles performed, effects produced) and is not construed as a single punctual occurrence without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The gram is morphologically marked as progressive perfect passive ('had been done'), which entails dynamic eventive unfolding and is incompatible with stative readings; additionally, the verb 'do' denotes a bounded dynamic event involving change and internal development.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram occurs in the protasis of a counterfactual conditional ('If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon...') paired with the apodosis 'they would have repented long ago', indicating a situation contrary to fact—the mighty works were not done in those cities, only in the addressees' cities.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been done' appears in a conditional clause ('If the mighty works had been done...') where negation is not applied to the auxiliary; the negation of the overall conditional is expressed through the counterfactual structure, not through direct negation of the target gram itself.
DtRes_explanation: The clause is counterfactual ('If the mighty works had been done...') and negates the actual occurrence of the event; no transition to a resulting state is entailed in reality.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes discrete bounded events (miracles performed in specific locations) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the counterfactual conditional structure focuses on the occurrence of these events as distinct facts, not their duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause is negated with a counterfactual conditional ('If...had been done...would have repented'), indicating the transition did not occur at the reference time and the situation remains unrealized, thus not presenting a bounded completed event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been done' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('If the mighty works had been done...they would have repented'), which presents the situation as hypothetical and non-realized rather than asserted as fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had been done' contains the morphologically past auxiliary 'had' followed by 'been' and a past participle, instantiating both past perfect and progressive perfect features, which satisfies the set_to_1_if conditions.
VcPass_explanation: The target instantiates VcPass: it has the form 'had been done' (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of transitive verb 'do'), the grammatical subject 'mighty works' is the semantic patient (entity affected by the action), and the agent is omitted, satisfying all criteria for passive voice morphology.

FullText: » Woe to you Chorazin ! Woe to you Bethsaida ! If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon , which were done in you , they would have repented long ago , sitting in sackcloth and ashes .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'repented' does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it is used intransitively without an affected patient syntactically realized.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'repented' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'they' who perform the action of repenting), making it unergative/transitive rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'repented' takes a grammatical subject ('they') functioning as an external argument (agent engaged in a volitional activity) and does not license or realize an internal argument (direct or indirect object).
AktAcc_explanation: Repent denotes a punctual change-of-state (transition into a state of contrition) without an inherent durative process phase; the event is a boundary transition into a mental/spiritual state rather than a gradual culminating process.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'repented' denotes a punctual transition into a new cognitive/spiritual state (from unrepentant to repentant), which is a change-of-state achievement that occurs at a definable threshold, despite the accompanying durative modifier 'long ago' and the participial phrase describing the manner of repentance.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'repent' denotes a bounded cognitive/emotional event marking a change of mind or state, which constitutes a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation with sustained engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'repented' entails a transition into a new resulting state (a change from unrepentant to repentant condition), which disqualifies it from being semelfactive according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'would have repented' denotes a bounded change-of-state event (transition from non-repentance to repentance) involving dynamic progression, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'would have repented' occurs in the apodosis (result clause) of a counterfactual conditional introduced by 'If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon', expressing an unfulfilled past condition and its contrary-to-fact consequence.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'would have repented' is not negated; it appears in a counterfactual conditional expressing what would have happened if the condition were met, but no negator (not, n't, never, or negative quantifier) directly negates this eventuality.
DtRes_explanation: The clause is counterfactual (conditional 'if' with past perfect antecedent) and does not describe an actual change of state that holds at the reference time; it expresses a hypothetical outcome that did not occur.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete hypothetical event (repentance as a transition into a new state) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and the counterfactual conditional structure focuses on the occurrence of the event itself, not on duration or habituality.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'would have repented' presents a transition event (repent = change of state) as a bounded, completed whole in a counterfactual conditional, construing the transition itself as a single accomplished occurrence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'would have repented' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('If the mighty works had been done...they would have repented'), presenting a hypothetical, non-realized situation contrary to fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'would have repented' includes the modal auxiliary 'would' preceding 'have', which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for modal perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would have repented' is active voice with a modal auxiliary and perfect aspect; the grammatical subject 'they' is the semantic agent (doer) performing the action of repenting, not the patient, and there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle structure).

FullText: » Behold , I have given you authority to tread upon serpents and scorpions , and over all the power of the enemy : and nothing shall in any way hurt you .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have given' introduces an overt grammatical subject (I), an overt direct object (authority), and an overt indirect object (you), instantiating a ditransitive pattern that satisfies the set_to_1_if condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have given' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'I') and introduces a direct object ('you authority'), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the clause contains an overt direct object introduced by the target verb.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have given' licenses and realizes an internal argument ('authority'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'give' denotes a punctual transfer of authority without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it is a boundary transition event that occurs instantaneously rather than unfolding over measurable duration toward completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have given' denotes a punctual transfer of authority into a new state, representing an instantaneous transition that can be modified by a specific time-point adverbial and does not permit interruption as an ongoing process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have given' denotes a completed transfer of authority with a specific result state (you now possess authority), entailing a defined endpoint rather than a dynamic durative situation.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have given (you) authority' entails a resulting change of state—a transition from not having authority to possessing authority—which disqualifies it from semelfactive status.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have given' denotes a completed transfer of authority (change-of-state event with a bounded endpoint), not a static condition that holds without dynamic change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have given you authority' presents the situation as actually occurring in the actual world (a completed action in the speaker's past), with no indication of counterfactuality, contrary-to-fact condition, or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have given (you) authority' is not negated; the negation 'nothing shall in any way hurt you' applies to a different eventuality (hurt), not to the giving of authority.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have given' describes a change of possession/status (granting authority), and the clause entails that this authority is currently held and in effect at the reference time, as evidenced by the subsequent clause 'to tread upon serpents and scorpions' which describes the active exercise of that authority.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'have given' denotes a completed transfer of authority (a transition into a new state), not a situation holding continuously over time; there is no duration adverbial or indication that the state of having authority extends continuously from past to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'I have given you authority' presents a completed transfer of authority as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint, instantiating the perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have given (you) authority' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world (present perfect indicating a completed action), not presented as projected, hypothetical, or non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have given' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'given' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'I' is the semantic agent (doer) who gives authority, and 'you' is the grammatical object functioning as the semantic patient; this is active voice, not passive voice with 'have been' + past participle morphology.

FullText: » All things have been delivered to me from my Father . No one knows who the Son is except the Father . And no one knows who the Father is except the Son and those to whom the Son is willing to reveal him . «
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('have been delivered'), and the lexical verb 'deliver' licenses both an external argument (agent: 'my Father') and an internal argument (patient: 'All things'), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('have been delivered'), and the lexical verb 'deliver' licenses an external argument (the Father as the implicit agent), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for passive clauses.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'delivered' licenses an internal argument ('all things' as the theme/patient), and in the passive construction 'have been delivered', the subject functions as the internal argument (patient) rather than an external argument (agent), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-state/transfer events with internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'delivered' denotes a punctual transfer or boundary transition (movement of things from Father to Son) without a durative process phase; it is not naturally modified by 'in X time' to measure completion duration, and the event itself lacks an inherent endpoint or result state distinct from the transfer itself.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'delivered' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (things passing from the Father to the Son), representing a definable threshold crossing that can be modified by specific time-point adverbials and does not permit interruption as an ongoing process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb phrase 'have been delivered' denotes a completed transfer with an inherent endpoint and result state (things are now in a state of having been delivered), satisfying the set_to_0_if criterion of entailing a specific, defined result state.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have been delivered' entails a resulting change of state (things transitioning from not-delivered to delivered), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have been delivered' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect ('have been' + past participle), which entails dynamic eventive unfolding and change-of-state (things being transferred from Father to subject), incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents 'have been delivered' as a factual statement about what has actually occurred in the actual world, with no indication of counterfactuality, conditional structure, or unfulfilled wishes.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have been delivered' is not negated; the sentence affirms that all things have been delivered to the speaker from the Father.
DtRes_explanation: The passive construction 'have been delivered' describes a change of possession (things transferred from Father to Son) where the resulting state of possession holds at the reference time, satisfying the set_to_1_if criterion for change of possession with current status held.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a completed transfer of discrete entities (things delivered from Father to Son) as a bounded event with focus on the transition/result, not a continuous situation holding over time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The passive form 'have been delivered' presents a bounded transition event (delivery as a completed whole) with a construed endpoint, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for passive bounded transitions.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have been delivered' is in the present perfect tense asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have been delivered' includes 'been' followed by a progressive participle ('delivered'), matching the set_to_1_if criterion for progressive perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have been delivered' instantiates the passive voice: it has the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle 'delivered' of a transitive verb, the grammatical subject 'All things' corresponds to the semantic patient (the entity affected by the delivery event), and the agent is expressed in a 'by'-phrase ('from my Father').

FullText: » However one thing is needed . Mary has chosen the proper way and it shall not be taken away from her . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has chosen' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('Mary') and an overt direct object ('the proper way'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has chosen' licenses an external argument (Mary as agent) and introduces a direct object (the proper way), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria for verbs with external arguments and overt objects.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has chosen' licenses an internal argument ('the proper way' as direct object), therefore it does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'choose' denotes a punctual, near-instantaneous decision or boundary transition into a chosen state, lacking a durative process phase that unfolds toward completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has chosen' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the decision/selection itself), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials (e.g., 'has chosen at that moment'), does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase, and lacks measurable internal duration, representing a boundary transition rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has chosen' denotes a punctual, bounded event with an inherent endpoint (the moment of choice completion) and a defined result state (having made a choice), not a dynamic durative situation.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has chosen' entails a transition into a resulting state (Mary is now in a state of having made a choice), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has chosen' denotes a bounded change-of-state event (the act of selecting/deciding) with a clear endpoint, involving dynamic eventive unfolding rather than a static condition that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'Mary has chosen the proper way' presents a situation as having actually occurred in the actual world without any counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication of unfulfilled wishes.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has chosen' is not negated; the negation 'shall not be taken away' applies to a different eventuality (the taking away), not to the choosing itself.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has chosen' describes a change of status/decision (Mary's selection of the proper way), and the resulting state (her choice) persists at the reference time, as evidenced by the consequent clause 'it shall not be taken away from her' which confirms the choice remains in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has chosen' denotes a discrete, bounded event (a single act of choosing) resulting in a new state, not a continuous situation or habitual series extending to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has chosen' is a transition event (selecting/deciding) construed as a completed whole, presenting a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has chosen' asserts Mary's action as a realized fact in the actual world, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has chosen' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'chosen' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'Mary' corresponds to the semantic agent (the one doing the choosing), and 'the proper way' is the grammatical object (semantic patient); this is active voice, not passive voice with the required 'have + been + past participle' morphology.

FullText: » If I use the finger power Spirit of God to cast out demons , then the kingdom of God has come upon you ?
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has come' introduces an overt internal argument 'upon you' (prepositional phrase functioning as a directional complement indicating the affected participant), satisfying the transitive pattern of a two-participant event directed toward an internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument (kingdom of God) undergoes displacement as an internal argument, and no external argument is licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' licenses an internal argument ('upon you'), and denotes a change-of-location/appearance event where the kingdom functions as the external argument but the prepositional phrase 'upon you' functions as a locative complement, making this a change-of-state event rather than a simple unergative activity.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a punctual boundary transition into a new state (arrival/presence of the kingdom) without a durative process phase, matching the achievement pattern of 'arrive' rather than an accomplishment with measurable duration toward a goal.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has come upon' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival/onset of the kingdom), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'has come upon you at that moment'), and lacks measurable internal duration, representing a boundary crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has come upon you' denotes a punctual event with an inherent endpoint (the arrival/onset of the kingdom), marking a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has come upon you' entails a transition into a new resulting state (the kingdom of God arriving/being present), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a change-of-state event marking the onset or arrival of the kingdom of God, involving dynamic progression from a prior state to a new state, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'the kingdom of God has come upon you' presents a situation as actually occurring (in the consequent of an open conditional), not as contrary to fact; the present perfect indicates a real result that follows from the condition, not an unfulfilled or counterfactual scenario.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has come' is not negated; the negator 'not' in the sentence applies to the embedded clause 'not to go' (implied in the conditional structure), not to the target gram itself.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'come' describes arrival at a destination (upon you), and the resulting state of the kingdom being present/arrived persists at the reference time, satisfying the resultative feature.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the arrival/establishment of the kingdom of God) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and there are no duration adverbials indicating continuous extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has come' is a transition event (arrival/occurrence) construed as a completed whole, presenting the kingdom of God's arrival as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has come' appears in the consequent of a conditional construction ('If...then the kingdom of God has come upon you'), which projects a hypothetical outcome dependent on the condition, marking it as irrealis rather than asserted fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has come' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'come', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has come' lacks passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle); 'come' is an intransitive verb with the grammatical subject 'kingdom of God' as the semantic agent/experiencer, not a patient, and there is no by-phrase.

FullText: » Woe to you Pharisees ! You tithe mint and rue and every herb , and pass over justice and the love of God . You should have done these but you leave them undone .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'done' introduces an overt direct object 'these', satisfying the condition for a two-participant transitive construction with an external argument (implied 'you') and an internal argument (the object 'these').
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'you') and takes a direct object ('these'), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition for clauses containing overt grammatical objects.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an internal argument ('these'), which functions as a direct object, thus failing the requirement for ASUnrg that no internal argument be licensed or realized.
AktAcc_explanation: The modal construction 'should have done these' denotes a durative process of performing multiple actions (the things mentioned: justice, love of God) directed toward a defined goal state of completion, fitting the accomplishment profile.
AktAch_explanation: The modal construction 'should have done' expresses a counterfactual obligation or recommendation rather than a punctual transition into a new state; it lacks the instantaneous boundary-crossing semantics required for AktAch classification.
AktAct_explanation: The modal construction 'should have done these' denotes a bounded, completed action with a defined result state (doing justice and love of God), not a dynamic durative activity without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'should have done (these)' refers to performing justice and love of God, which entails a resulting change of state or condition rather than a single punctual occurrence without consequence.
AktState_explanation: The modal construction 'should have done' denotes a bounded, counterfactual event with internal dynamic development (the action of doing), marking a change-of-state from not-done to done, which is incompatible with stative aspect.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'should have done' occurs in a clause expressing an unfulfilled obligation or wish ('You should have done these but you leave them undone'), where the event is understood not to have occurred, marked by the contrastive 'but' indicating the action was not performed.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'should have done' is not negated; the negation applies to the separate clause 'you leave them undone' which expresses what actually occurs, not to the modal construction itself.
DtRes_explanation: The modal 'should have done' expresses a counterfactual obligation or recommendation about a past action that was not performed; it does not describe an actual change of state that holds at the reference time, but rather a failure to transition to a desired state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'You should have done these' expresses a counterfactual obligation regarding discrete actions (doing justice and love of God) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual markers indicating continuous extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The modal 'should have done' expresses a counterfactual obligation or unrealized expectation rather than presenting a bounded completed event; it does not assert that the transition occurred as a single bounded whole, but rather that it ought to have occurred but did not.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The modal auxiliary 'should' combined with the perfect infinitive 'have done' presents the situation as a hypothetical obligation or counterfactual evaluation (what ought to have been done but was not), rather than as a realized fact in the actual world.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'should have done' includes the modal auxiliary 'should' preceding 'have', which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for modal perfect forms.

FullText: » That which you have said in the darkness will be heard in the light . That which you have spoken in the inner chambers will be proclaimed on the housetops .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have said' introduces an overt internal argument ('that which' / the content clause functioning as direct object), satisfying the criterion for a two-participant transitive construction with both external and internal arguments.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'said' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'you' who performs the action of saying), and the clause contains an overt direct object ('that which' / the content said), so it does not meet the criteria for unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have said' licenses an internal argument (the content of what was said, realized as a relative clause 'that which you have said'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have said' denotes a punctual speech act that constitutes a boundary transition into the state of having uttered words, lacking a durative process phase directed toward a defined endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have said' denotes a punctual transition into a new state of utterance (the crossing of the threshold from silence to speech), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials ('said at midnight'), does not permit interruption as an ongoing process ('one cannot stop saying'), and lacks measurable internal duration, thus instantiating AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have said' denotes a completed speech act with a defined result state (utterance completed), not a dynamic durative situation; it lacks the homogeneous property of activities and cannot naturally take 'for X time' modification without implying iterative punctual events rather than a single ongoing action.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have said' entails a resulting change of state (words uttered in darkness will be heard in light), and the perfect aspect construes it as a durative situation with consequent results rather than a single punctual occurrence without change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have said' denotes a bounded perceptual/communicative event marking the completion of speech, which is a dynamic change-of-state event rather than a static condition holding over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a situation as having actually occurred in the actual world (things that have been said in darkness), without any indication of counterfactuality, unfulfilled wishes, or conditional reasoning.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have said' is not negated; the negator 'not' does not appear in the clause, and no negative quantifiers, 'never', or 'neither…nor' constructions negate this eventuality.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have said' is a communication verb that merely reports a past utterance without entailing that the information or directive is currently in effect; the clause describes what was said in the past without indicating a persisting resulting state at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes discrete bounded speech acts (saying in darkness, speaking in inner chambers) that will have specific consequences, focusing on the events themselves rather than a situation holding continuously over time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'you have said in the darkness' presents the act of saying as a completed, bounded event (a transition from not-said to said) that functions as a discrete step in the narrative sequence, meeting the criteria for perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have said' is in the present perfect tense asserting a realized action in the actual world without modal, conditional, future, or wish contexts; the subsequent clause 'will be heard' projects a future consequence, but the target gram itself asserts the prior speech act as a completed fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have said' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have said' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'you' as the semantic agent (doer) and lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: » That which you have said in the darkness will be heard in the light . That which you have spoken in the inner chambers will be proclaimed on the housetops .

AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have spoken' denotes a durative communicative act that unfolds over measurable time rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and it can naturally be modified by duration adverbials (e.g., 'have spoken for hours'), failing the achievement criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have spoken' denotes a completed perceptual/communicative event with an inherent endpoint (the act of speaking is finished), lacks dynamic durative unfolding, and cannot naturally take a 'for X time' adverbial without implying iterative repetition rather than a single activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have spoken' entails a resulting change of state (words uttered in the past that now exist as a completed action with consequences), and the present perfect construction indicates a durative or completed action rather than a single punctual occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have spoken' denotes a bounded perceptual/communicative event marking the completion of speech, which involves dynamic eventive unfolding and change-of-state rather than a static condition holding over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'That which you have spoken in the inner chambers' presents a situation as having actually occurred (using present perfect to describe real past speech), without any indication of counterfactuality, contrary-to-fact condition, or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have spoken' is not negated; the negation in the sentence ('will be heard', 'will be proclaimed') applies to different predicates, not to the target gram itself.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have spoken' reports a past communication event without entailing that the spoken words are currently binding or in effect; the clause merely describes what was said in the past, similar to the negative example 'He has given me advice before.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes discrete bounded events (things spoken in inner chambers) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, with no duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.

TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have spoken' is in the present perfect tense asserting a realized action in the actual world (what you have spoken), with no modal auxiliary, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have spoken' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'spoken' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.

FullText: » I will show you the one to fear . Fear the one who , after he has killed , has power to destroy you throw into the ever-burning trash fires of the valley of Hinnom Greek : Gehenna . This is one you should fear .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has killed' lacks an overt internal argument (direct object); the patient of the killing action is not syntactically realized, making this an activity reading without an affected object.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'killed' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'he' who performs the killing action), and the clause contains an implicit direct object (the patient/theme being killed), so it does not meet the criteria for unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has killed' licenses an internal argument (the implied object 'you' or a general patient), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has killed' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous change-of-state (transition from alive to dead) without a durative process phase, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for achievement verbs.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has killed' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (death of the victim) that occurs at a definable temporal boundary, satisfying the core criterion for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has killed' denotes a completed action with an inherent endpoint and result state (someone is dead), satisfying the set_to_0_if criterion of entailing a specific, defined result state.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has killed' entails a resulting change of state (transition from alive to dead) and is not construed as a single punctual occurrence without consequent result, thus it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has killed' denotes a bounded dynamic event involving change-of-state (transition from alive to dead), which is incompatible with AktState despite the perfect aspect framing.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'after he has killed' presents a situation as actually occurring (a completed action in the past) within a factual narrative context, not as contrary to fact or unfulfilled.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has killed' is not negated; the negation in the sentence ('not to fear', 'should fear') applies to different elements, and 'has killed' describes a realized event in a conditional clause.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has killed' entails a change of state (the victim is dead) that persists at the reference time, and this resulting state is relevant for the subsequent clause 'has power to destroy you,' making it non-experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has killed' describes a past action whose resulting state (death) does not persist at the reference time in the context of the threat; the clause emphasizes the agent's subsequent power to destroy, not the persistence of the killing itself.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'after he has killed' describes a discrete bounded event (the act of killing) rather than a situation holding continuously over time; the focus is on the completion of the action as a prerequisite condition, not on duration or habituality.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has killed' is a transition event (kill = change of state from alive to dead) construed as a completed whole in a narrative sequence ('after he has killed, has power to destroy'), presenting a bounded occurrence rather than an ongoing or stative situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has killed' asserts a realized action within the conditional protasis 'after he has killed'; it describes an actual completed event as a precondition for the consequent, not a projected or hypothetical situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has killed' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'killed' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has killed' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'he' as the semantic agent (doer) and lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: » Know this , that if the master of the house had known in what hour the thief was coming he would have watched . He would not have allowed his house to be broken into .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had known' introduces an overt internal argument ('in what hour the thief was coming'), satisfying the transitive pattern of an external argument (master) directing an action toward an internal argument (the content clause).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'know' licenses an external argument (the master as agent/experiencer) and takes a direct object ('in what hour the thief was coming'), thus it is not unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'known' licenses an internal argument (the implied object 'what hour the thief was coming'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'had known' denotes a punctual cognitive achievement—a single transition into a state of awareness or understanding—rather than a durative process culminating in a defined endpoint, and thus lacks the inherent process phase required for AktAcc.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had known' denotes a punctual cognitive transition into awareness or recognition (learning/discovering information at a specific moment), which is a classic achievement verb that can be modified by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'had known at that hour') and does not permit interruption as an ongoing process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had known' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of awareness (knowing a specific fact about when the thief was coming), which is treated as a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had known' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not knowing to knowing), which disqualifies it from being a semelfactive; additionally, it is a stative verb rather than a dynamic punctual occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had known' denotes a mental state (knowledge) that would hold over time without dynamic change or progression, satisfying the stative condition despite its counterfactual conditional context.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram occurs in the protasis of a counterfactual conditional ('if the master of the house had known') paired with apodosis containing modal perfect ('would have watched'), expressing a situation contrary to fact where the master did not actually know the hour of the thief's coming.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had known' is in a conditional clause ('if the master of the house had known') and is not itself negated; the negation ('would not have allowed') applies to a different verb in the consequent clause, not to the target gram.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'had known' functions as a claim about the absence of an event in the subject's (master's) history—he did not have the experience of knowing when the thief was coming, which is characteristic of the experiential perfect.
DtRes_explanation: The clause is counterfactual (introduced by 'if') and negates the actual occurrence of the event; no transition to a resulting state is entailed in reality.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a hypothetical transition into knowledge at a specific point in time ('in what hour'), focusing on the moment of coming to know rather than a situation holding continuously over a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'know' denotes a stative situation (possession of knowledge) without a construed boundary or transition, and the clause does not present a bounded occurrence but rather a hypothetical condition in a counterfactual construction.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had known' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('if the master of the house had known...he would have watched'), which presents a hypothetical, non-realized situation rather than an asserted fact.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, forming a past perfect construction ('had known'), which instantiates TLTS according to the set_to_1_if rule for past auxiliary marking.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had known' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'the master' as the semantic agent (doer) and lacks the passive morphology 'have/had + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: » Know this , that if the master of the house had known in what hour the thief was coming he would have watched . He would not have allowed his house to be broken into .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'watched' licenses an external argument (the master as agent/initiator who performs the action of watching), which violates the unaccusative requirement that no external argument be licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'watched' licenses an internal argument (the implied object 'his house' or the event being monitored), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'would have watched' denotes a durative process of vigilant monitoring directed toward the goal of preventing theft, naturally modifiable by 'in X time' (e.g., 'watched in that hour'), and involves sustained action culminating in a defined protective result state.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'watched' denotes a durative activity (maintaining vigilance over a period of time) rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and it cannot be naturally modified by a specific time-point adverbial in this context.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'would have watched' denotes a dynamic, durative situation of sustained vigilance that unfolds over time without an inherent endpoint; it can be naturally modified by 'for X time' (e.g., 'would have watched for hours') and is homogeneous in nature, satisfying the criteria for AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'watched' entails a durative state of vigilance/monitoring over a period of time rather than a single punctual occurrence, and does not fit the semelfactive profile of instantaneous bounded events like 'knock' or 'sneeze'.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'would have watched' denotes a dynamic activity involving active volitional engagement and internal progression (monitoring/surveillance over time), which constitutes eventive unfolding rather than a static state.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'would have watched' occurs in the apodosis (result clause) of a counterfactual conditional introduced by 'if the master of the house had known', expressing an unfulfilled past action contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'would have watched' is not negated; the negation 'would not have allowed' appears in the following clause and negates a different eventuality.
DtExp_explanation: The counterfactual conditional 'would have watched' presents a hypothetical event as something the subject would have undergone/experienced (a bounded activity of vigilance), focusing on the occurrence of the experience rather than a resulting state that persists at reference time.
DtRes_explanation: The target is a counterfactual conditional ('would have watched') that does not describe an actual change of state; it expresses a hypothetical action that did not occur, so no resulting state holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a hypothetical discrete action (watching at a specific time) that would have occurred in response to a condition, rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'would have watched' presents a bounded transition event (watching as a completed action) with a construed endpoint in a counterfactual conditional structure, instantiating perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'would have watched' appears in a conditional construction ('if the master of the house had known...he would have watched'), presenting a counterfactual/hypothetical situation rather than a realized fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'would have watched' includes a modal auxiliary ('would') preceding 'have', which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for modal perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would have watched' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'he' as the semantic agent (doer) and 'watched' as a transitive verb taking an implicit object; it lacks the passive morphology of 'have been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: » Know this , that if the master of the house had known in what hour the thief was coming he would have watched . He would not have allowed his house to be broken into .

AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'allow' denotes a punctual decision or permission event without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it is a boundary transition into a state of permission rather than a gradual change-of-state or directed action toward completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'allowed' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (preventing/blocking an action at a specific moment), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials ('would not have allowed at that hour'), and represents a boundary transition rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'allowed' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event with an inherent endpoint (preventing the breaking-in), not a dynamic durative activity; it entails a specific result state and lacks homogeneous unfolding.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'allowed his house to be broken into' entails a resulting change of state (the house being broken into), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'allowed' denotes a dynamic volitional action (permitting or preventing an event) that involves active engagement and change-of-state semantics; the conditional perfect construction 'would not have allowed' marks a bounded eventive scenario, not a static condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'would not have allowed' occurs in the apodosis (result clause) of a counterfactual conditional, expressing what would have happened under the unfulfilled condition 'if the master of the house had known in what hour the thief was coming'.
ClNegPol_explanation: The auxiliary 'would' is negated with 'not' in the form 'would not have allowed', directly negating the eventuality of allowing the house to be broken into.

DtRes_explanation: This is a counterfactual conditional (would not have allowed) describing a hypothetical past scenario that did not occur; it does not entail an actual change of state or resulting state holding at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete hypothetical event (preventing a house break-in) rather than a situation holding continuously over time; it expresses a transition into a preventive state focused on a specific bounded occurrence, not a continuous duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause presents a counterfactual bounded transition (preventing/not allowing a break-in as a completed whole event) with a construed endpoint, fitting the perfective aspect profile despite the conditional mood.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'would not have allowed' is a modal auxiliary construction (would + have + allowed) in a counterfactual conditional context ('if the master...had known'), presenting the situation as hypothetical and unrealized rather than asserted as fact.

FullText: » Do you think I have come to give peace in the earth ? I tell you , No , instead division .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'come' does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it is an intransitive verb used without an affected patient realized syntactically.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument (I) undergoes displacement, and no external argument is licensed, matching the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a change-of-location event where the sole argument (I) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing movement to a location, not as an external argument initiating an activity.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a punctual boundary transition (arrival at a location or state) without a durative process phase, matching the achievement pattern exemplified by 'arrive'.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival at a location or in a role), which is a classic achievement verb that marks a definable threshold moment and can be modified by specific time-point adverbials (e.g., 'have come at dawn').
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event with an inherent endpoint (arrival at a location or state), not a dynamic durative activity; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without changing meaning, and represents completion of a goal rather than ongoing action.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have come' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not being present to being present), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a change-of-state event involving dynamic progression from not having arrived to having arrived, which is a bounded eventive action incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents the situation as actually occurring in the actual world (a rhetorical question about the speaker's actual purpose), without any counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the event is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'No' in the response clause negates the proposition about giving peace, not the target gram 'have come' in the question, which remains syntactically positive.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have come' entails a change of location (arrival on earth) where the resulting state of being present is relevant to the subsequent discourse about the purpose of that arrival, making it non-experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'come' describes arrival at a destination, but the speaker is asking a rhetorical question about purpose ('to give peace'), not asserting that the mover has arrived and remains at a destination; moreover, the context indicates the speaker is denying the purpose rather than confirming a persisting resultant state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (coming to give peace) with focus on the transition/purpose rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for verbs entailing transitions.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'come' is a transition event denoting movement/arrival, and 'have come' presents this transition as a completed whole bounded event rather than ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have come' is embedded in a rhetorical question that asserts a realized fact about the speaker's actual purpose, not a projected or hypothetical situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have come' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'come' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The form 'have come' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); 'come' is a past participle but 'been' is absent, and the grammatical subject 'I' functions as the semantic agent (doer), not the patient.

FullText: » So he told his gardener , I have been coming here looking for figs on this tree for three years . I have not found any . So cut it down ! Why should it continue using the soil ?

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' licenses an external argument (the speaker 'I') as an agent performing directed motion intentionally, not undergoing displacement as a theme/patient, thus failing the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'coming' denotes manner of motion but licenses an internal argument (the prepositional phrase 'here' functions as a locational complement indicating the goal/destination of the motion), and the full construction 'have been coming here' realizes a change-of-location event with a specified endpoint, thus failing the ASUnrg criterion.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'have been coming here' denotes a durative process (repeated action over three years) directed toward a defined goal (looking for figs), naturally modified by 'for three years' to measure the bounded duration of the accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The present perfect continuous 'have been coming here' denotes a durative activity unfolding over measurable duration ('for three years'), not a punctual transition into a new state.
AktAct_explanation: The verb phrase 'have been coming here' denotes a dynamic, durative situation of repeated visits over an extended period (three years), which is homogeneous and naturally modified by 'for three years', satisfying the criteria for an activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The present perfect progressive 'have been coming here' construes the event as a durative activity unfolding over measurable duration ('for three years'), not as a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the semelfactive criterion.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have been coming here' is morphologically marked as progressive aspect ('have been' + present participle), which entails dynamic unfolding and is explicitly incompatible with stative readings per the set_to_0_if criteria.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a factual situation that has actually occurred in the actual world (the speaker has genuinely been coming to the tree for three years), with no indication that it is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have been coming here' is not negated; the negation in the text ('I have not found any') applies to a different clause and does not negate the eventuality of coming.
DtExp_explanation: The clause contains the duration adverbial 'for three years', which indicates a continuing situation rather than a completed life experience, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The clause contains a duration adverbial ('for three years'), indicating a continuing habitual/iterative situation rather than a completed transition to a resulting state that holds at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause contains 'for three years' with a numerical bounded count, indicating distinct repeated occurrences rather than a continuous situation holding throughout the time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have been coming here' has the form 'have been' + -ing (progressive), which profiles the internal temporal development of the situation rather than presenting it as a bounded whole, and is reinforced by the duration adverbial 'for three years' that makes the internal temporal span accessible.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have been coming here' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world (present perfect continuous describing a three-year habitual action), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have been coming here' includes 'been' followed by a progressive participle ('coming'), which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for progressive perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have been coming' is an active progressive (present perfect continuous), not a passive voice construction; the grammatical subject 'I' is the semantic agent performing the action of coming, and there is no past participle in passive function.

FullText: » So he told his gardener , I have been coming here looking for figs on this tree for three years . I have not found any . So cut it down ! Why should it continue using the soil ?
