DtRes_explanation: The verb 'see' is a perception event that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; it reports a past experiential event without a change of state or current effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded state of non-occurrence (no one has seen) rather than a continuous situation holding throughout a time span, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual iteration markers required for DtUniv classification.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'see' is a transition/accomplishment verb construed as a completed bounded event (no one has achieved the state of having seen the Father), presenting the situation as a single bounded occurrence rather than ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has seen' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (no one in existence has seen the Father), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has seen' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'seen' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'no one' corresponds to the semantic agent (the one doing the seeing), and 'the Father' is the grammatical object (semantic patient); this is active voice, not passive voice with the required 'have + been + past participle' morphology.

FullText: » No one has seen the Father . Only he who is from God , He has seen the Father .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has seen' licenses an external argument (the subject 'he' as agent/experiencer) and introduces a direct object ('the Father'), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has seen' licenses an internal argument (direct object 'the Father'), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has seen' denotes a punctual perceptual event—a near-instantaneous transition into a state of visual awareness—without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, matching the achievement (0) classification for instantaneous cognitive/perceptual transitions.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has seen' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness or recognition (seeing the Father), which is a near-instantaneous crossing of a definable threshold from not-seeing to seeing, matching the positive example 'They have heard the news.'
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (seeing the Father), which is a single completed occurrence without durative unfolding, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for bounded perceptual events.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has seen' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not having seen to having seen the Father), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of visual awareness (seeing the Father), which encodes eventive change rather than a static stative condition, triggering the set_to_0_if criterion for bounded perceptual events.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'He has seen the Father' presents a situation as actually occurring in the actual world without any counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the event did not occur.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'He has seen' is not negated; the negation 'No one has seen' applies to a different clause with a different subject, and the target gram appears in a positive assertion ('Only he who is from God, He has seen the Father').
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has seen' denotes perception where the focus is on the occurrence of the experience (having witnessed the Father) at least once in the subject's life history, rather than on a resulting state, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for perception verbs.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has seen' is a perception event that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; it reports a past perceptual experience without a change of state or current binding condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (seeing the Father) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, with no duration adverbials or iterative framing indicating continuous extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has seen' is a transition event (perception/discovery) construed as a completed whole, presenting a bounded occurrence rather than an ongoing or stative situation.

FullText: » You go to this feast . I will not go to this feast for my time has not yet come . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'come' functions as an intransitive verb with no overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); 'time' is the grammatical subject, not an object, and the clause describes a state/event without an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument (time) undergoes displacement as an internal argument, with no external argument licensed, matching the unaccusative pattern.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a change-of-location/appearance event where the sole argument 'time' functions as an internal argument (theme) rather than an external argument, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for unaccusative verbs like 'fall'.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'time has not yet come' denotes a punctual boundary transition into a state (the arrival of a moment in time) without a durative process phase, matching the achievement profile rather than accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival at a defined temporal boundary), naturally modified by the time-point adverbial 'yet' and the negation, indicating a threshold moment when the time will arrive.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'time has not yet come' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of a specific moment or condition, lacking dynamic unfolding and requiring no sustained engagement; it entails a defined endpoint (when the time does come) rather than an ongoing activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'has not yet come' is a stative predicate describing a resulting state condition (time not having arrived), not a punctual dynamic event without change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'has not yet come' denotes a state of non-arrival or non-occurrence that holds at the reference time; it expresses a static relational condition (time existing but not having arrived) without dynamic change or eventive unfolding.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'my time has not yet come' presents a factual situation in the actual world (the speaker's time genuinely has not yet arrived), not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish, so it does not instantiate ClCntfcl.
ClNegPol_explanation: The auxiliary 'has' is directly negated with 'not' in the target gram 'has not yet come', which instantiates ClNegPol according to the first set_to_1_if condition.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'come' entails a change of state/transition to a new temporal point, and the resulting state (time not yet arrived) holds at the reference time, making this non-experiential rather than a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'my time has not yet come' is negated ('has not yet come'), so no transition to a resulting state is entailed; the event has not occurred, and therefore no change of state holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'has not yet come' denotes a transition into a new state (the arrival of time) rather than a situation holding continuously over a time span, and lacks duration adverbials indicating continuous extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'has not yet come' is negated with 'not yet', indicating the transition has not occurred and the situation remains open, per the set_to_0_if criterion.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has not yet come' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (the time has not arrived as a present fact), and negation alone does not trigger irrealis; the clause is embedded as a causal justification for a future decision, not as a modal, conditional, or hypothetical construction.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has not yet come' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'come' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'has' is 'come' (a past participle functioning as a lexical verb in active voice), not a passive construction; 'time' is the semantic agent/subject of the event, and there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle sequence).

FullText: The Jews wondered saying : » How does this man know letters ? He has had no formal education . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has had' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('He') and an overt direct object ('no formal education'), satisfying the transitive pattern required for ASTrns=1.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have' licenses an external argument (the subject 'He') as an agent/possessor, and the clause contains a direct object 'no formal education', thus failing the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had' licenses an internal argument ('no formal education'), which is a direct object, thus failing the requirement for ASUnrg that no internal argument be licensed or realized.
AktAcc_explanation: The target describes a stative condition (lack of formal education) rather than a dynamic event with a durative process leading to a defined endpoint; it denotes a persistent state without inherent culmination or measurable completion.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'has had no formal education' denotes a state or condition persisting over an extended duration rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and lacks the instantaneous boundary-crossing characteristic required for AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'has had no formal education' denotes a stative result state (lack of education) without dynamic unfolding or sustained engagement, and it lacks the homogeneity and durative character required for AktAct classification.
AktSemel_explanation: The target is a stative predicate expressing a persistent condition (lack of formal education) rather than a dynamic punctual event, so it does not instantiate the semelfactive feature.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has had no formal education' denotes a stable possessional/existential state (lack of education) that holds over time without dynamic change or internal progression, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for existence/possession and static relational configuration.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a factual statement about the man's actual educational background without any counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the situation is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has had no formal education' contains the negative quantifier 'no' in object position, which negates the eventuality of having formal education, making the situation absent from reality.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'has had no formal education' functions as a claim about the absence of an event in the subject's life history, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for negative Perfects.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'has had no formal education' is a stative copular construction describing a persistent condition rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state; it reports a state of affairs (lack of education) without entailing a change of state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'has had no formal education' denotes a state (absence of formal education) that has held continuously from a past starting point up to the reference time, matching the intuition of a non-terminating situation extending to the present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has had no formal education' denotes a stative situation (lack of education as an unbounded state) without a construed boundary or transition, and the clause does not present a bounded occurrence but rather an ongoing condition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has had no formal education' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world as a fact about the man, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has had' contains the auxiliary 'had' (past participle of 'have') following present-tense 'has', which is a present perfect with past marking that instantiates TLTS according to the rule for morphologically past auxiliaries in perfect constructions.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'has had no formal education' uses 'had' as a lexical verb (meaning 'possessed/received'), not as a passive auxiliary; the complement 'no formal education' is a noun phrase (not a past participle), making this a copular/possessive construction, not passive voice morphology.

FullText: They looked for a way to capture him . But no man laid his hand on him . This is because his hour had not yet arrived .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'arrived' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it only takes an external argument (the subject 'hour').
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'arrive' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument ('his hour') undergoes displacement, and no external argument is licensed, matching the set_to_1_if criteria for unaccusative verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'arrive' denotes a change-of-location event where the sole argument ('hour') functions as an internal argument (theme) undergoing a transition into a state, not as an external argument initiating an activity.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'arrive' denotes a punctual boundary transition into a state (the moment when an hour comes) without a durative process phase, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for achievement verbs.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'arrive' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the moment when 'his hour' comes), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials ('at that moment'), and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying the criteria for AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'arrive' denotes a punctual event with an inherent endpoint (the moment of arrival), and the negated perfect 'had not yet arrived' marks a bounded perceptual/temporal event indicating non-completion of a defined goal state, not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'arrive' entails a transition into a new resulting state (from not-arrived to arrived), which disqualifies it from being a semelfactive; additionally, the negation and perfect aspect construe this as a durative state rather than a punctual occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'arrive' denotes a change-of-state event with an inherent endpoint (the moment of arrival), and the perfect aspect 'had not yet arrived' marks the bounded temporal relation to that eventive boundary, making this incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'his hour had not yet arrived' presents a factual situation in the actual world (explaining why no one laid hands on him), using past perfect for temporal anteriority within narrative, not expressing a counterfactual condition.
ClNegPol_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is directly negated by 'not' in the target gram 'had not yet arrived', which presents the event of arrival as not occurring, satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'arrive' entails a change of state/transition to a new temporal point, and the resulting state (the hour not having come yet) is relevant to the discourse explaining why no one could capture him.
DtRes_explanation: The negated motion verb 'arrive' does not entail a resulting state; the clause states that the arrival has NOT occurred, so no transition to a destination state is entailed at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'had not yet arrived' denotes a transition into a new state (the arrival of an hour) and focuses on that transition rather than on a situation holding continuously over time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for verbs entailing state transitions.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause is negated with 'not yet', indicating the transition has not occurred at the reference time and the situation remains open, per the set_to_0_if criterion.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had not yet arrived' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (his hour had not arrived at that time), with negation and temporal positioning but no modal, conditional, future, or wish context.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past tense, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had not yet arrived' is an active intransitive verb (past perfect of 'arrive'), not a passive construction; it lacks the required 'have + been + past participle' morphology and the subject 'hour' is the semantic agent/experiencer, not a patient.

FullText: Many in the crowd believed in him . They asked : » When the Christ comes will he do more signs than those this man has done ? «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has done' introduces an overt internal argument ('signs' as the direct object in the comparative phrase 'more signs than those this man has done'), satisfying the transitive requirement.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'do' licenses an external argument (the agent 'this man') and introduces a direct object ('signs'), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria for verbs with external arguments and overt objects.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an internal argument ('signs' or 'more signs'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'do' in 'has done' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous action without an inherent durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it refers to completed discrete acts (signs) rather than a gradual change-of-state or directed process toward a goal.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has done' refers to a completed set of actions (signs/miracles) that unfold over measurable duration rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and the event can naturally be modified by 'finish' (e.g., 'finish doing signs'), indicating a durative process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has done' denotes a bounded, completed action with an inherent endpoint (performing signs), not a dynamic durative situation; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without changing meaning, and the internal argument 'signs' is quantized and bounded.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has done' (signs) entails a resulting change of state and refers to a completed action with consequent results, not a single punctual occurrence without change of state.
AktState_explanation: The present perfect 'has done' denotes a bounded dynamic event (performing signs/miracles) with internal eventive unfolding and a completion boundary, which instantiates dynamic change rather than a stative condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'this man has done' presents a situation that actually occurred in the actual world (the man performed signs), and occurs in an embedded interrogative introduced by 'if' (indirect question about future possibility), not in a counterfactual conditional.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has done' is not negated; it appears in a comparative question about signs and is affirmed as a realized event in the past.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'do signs' entails a change of state (miracles performed) where the resulting state of accomplished signs is relevant to the comparison being made in the discourse, not merely whether the experience occurred in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has done' refers to past signs/miracles performed by the man, but the clause does not entail that a resulting state from those actions persists at the reference time; it merely reports completed past actions without a current binding effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes discrete bounded events (signs performed) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the accomplishment of specific actions rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has done' presents a completed action (performing signs) as a bounded whole event, construed as a single accomplished occurrence rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has done' asserts a realized fact in the actual world (the signs this man has already performed), embedded within a comparative question but not itself marked as modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has done' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'done' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has done' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'this man' as the semantic agent (doer), not passive voice; it lacks the required auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle.

FullText: » No man has ever spoken the way he speaks , « they replied .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has spoken' does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it is used intransitively without an affected patient syntactically realized.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'spoken' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'man' who performs the action of speaking), and the clause contains an overt direct object 'the way', making this an agentive transitive construction rather than an unaccusative one.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has spoken' licenses an internal argument (the direct object 'the way'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'spoken' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous event of producing speech without an inherent durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint or result state; it cannot naturally be modified by 'in X time' to indicate completion of a bounded accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has spoken' denotes a durative communicative event that unfolds over measurable duration rather than a punctual transition into a new state; speaking is an activity with internal temporal structure, not a boundary-crossing achievement like 'arrive' or 'find'.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has spoken' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of awareness or a completed utterance with an inherent endpoint, not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without inherent completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The present perfect 'has spoken' construes the event as a durative activity with a resulting state (the fact of having spoken), not as a single punctual occurrence without change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has spoken' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the completion of a speech act (change-of-state from not having spoken to having spoken), which is eventive and incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'No man has ever spoken the way he speaks' presents a factual statement about what has actually occurred in the actual world, with no indication of counterfactuality, unfulfilled wishes, or conditional reasoning.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has spoken' is negated by the negative quantifier 'No man' in subject position, which construes the event as not occurring.
DtExp_explanation: The clause contains the explicit frequency adverb 'ever', which indicates a claim about whether an event has occurred at least once in the subject's life history, fitting the set_to_1_if criterion for frequency adverbs and the intuition of experiential aspect.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'speak' is a communication/activity verb that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; the clause reports a past interaction without indicating that any directive or information is currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (no man has ever spoken in this manner) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating continuous extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has spoken' is a transition event (speak as a completed utterance act) construed as a bounded whole, asserting a completed occurrence rather than an ongoing or habitual state.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has spoken' asserts a realized fact in the actual world (a past event that occurred), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has spoken' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'spoken' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has spoken' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of intransitive verb 'speak'), not passive voice; the grammatical subject 'No man' is the semantic agent (the speaker), not the patient.

FullText: » Does our law convict a man without first hearing from him to find out what he has done ? «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has done' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('he') and an overt direct object ('what'), satisfying the condition for ASTrns marking.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'done' (past participle of 'do') licenses an external argument (the man/him as agent) and denotes an agentive action without specifying a change-of-state or unaccusative event, thus failing all set_to_1 conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has done' licenses an internal argument (the implied object of what was done), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has done' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous completed action without an inherent durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it refers to a discrete event or action already completed, not a gradual change-of-state or directed process toward a goal.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has done' denotes a general completed action without specifying a punctual transition into a new state; it refers to an unspecified action or series of actions that unfolds over duration rather than a boundary-crossing achievement like 'arrive' or 'find'.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'what he has done' refers to a completed action or result state (the specific deed/wrongdoing) rather than an ongoing durative activity, making it a bounded accomplishment rather than an activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The verb 'has done' is a perfect aspect construction referring to a completed action with potential resulting state or consequence (what he has done = his actions and their outcomes), not a single punctual dynamic occurrence without change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has done' denotes a bounded dynamic event with internal eventive unfolding and change-of-state, involving active progression toward completion, which is incompatible with the stative feature AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'what he has done' is an embedded interrogative introduced by 'what' (indirect question), presenting an actual situation to be determined, not a counterfactual condition contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negator 'without' does not negate the target gram 'has done'; rather, it negates the manner of conviction, while 'has done' is embedded in a relative clause describing what the man has done.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'what he has done' focuses on the occurrence of an action as part of the subject's history without asserting a resulting state, answering whether an event has occurred at least once, which instantiates the experiential perfect (DtExp).
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has done' reports a past action without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; it is used to identify what actions the man performed, not to indicate a current state resulting from those actions.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'what he has done' describes a discrete bounded event (an action completed in the past) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, with no duration adverbials or habitual series present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'what he has done' presents a completed action as a bounded whole without internal temporal development, construing the situation as a single accomplished event rather than an ongoing or habitual state.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has done' asserts a realized state of affairs (what he has actually done) within a relative clause that provides factual information about the man, not a projected, hypothetical, or desired situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has done' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'done' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has done' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle 'done' of a transitive verb), where the grammatical subject 'he' is the semantic agent performing the action, not the patient; passive voice requires the form 'have been' + past participle.

FullText: He spoke these words at the treasury as he taught in the temple . No one tried to arrest him because his hour had not yet arrived .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'arrived' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it describes a one-participant event with only an external argument (his hour).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'arrive' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument ('his hour') undergoes a change in state without licensing an external argument, matching the set_to_1_if criteria for unaccusative verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'arrive' denotes a change-of-location event where the sole argument 'his hour' functions as an internal argument (theme) undergoing a change of state rather than as an external argument initiating an action.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'arrive' denotes a punctual boundary transition into a state (the moment when 'his hour' comes) without a durative process phase, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for achievement verbs.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'arrive' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the moment when 'his hour' comes), naturally modified by the time-point adverbial 'not yet', and represents a definable threshold crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'arrive' denotes a punctual event with an inherent endpoint (the moment of arrival), and 'had not yet arrived' marks a bounded perceptual/temporal event indicating non-completion of a defined goal state, not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'arrive' entails a transition into a new resulting state (from not-arrived to arrived), which disqualifies it from being a semelfactive; additionally, the perfect aspect 'has not yet arrived' construes this as a durative situation extending to the present moment.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'arrive' denotes a change-of-state event with an inherent endpoint (the moment of arrival); the perfect aspect 'has not yet arrived' marks the non-occurrence of this bounded dynamic event up to the reference time, which is incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'his hour had not yet arrived' presents a factual situation within the narrative (explaining why no one arrested him), using past perfect for temporal anteriority without any counterfactual marking or indication that the situation is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is directly negated by 'not' in 'had not yet arrived', which negates the eventuality of arrival itself according to the first set_to_1_if condition.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'arrive' entails a change of location/state, and the negated perfect 'had not yet arrived' asserts that this resulting state (the hour being present/at hand) does not hold at the reference time, making it a state-oriented rather than experiential reading.
DtRes_explanation: The negation 'has not yet arrived' entails that no transition to a resulting state has occurred; the subject has not moved to a destination and does not remain there, so no persisting change of state holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a transition into a new state (the arrival of 'his hour') rather than a situation holding continuously over time; the focus is on the non-occurrence of a punctual event, not on duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause is negated with 'not yet', indicating that the transition (arrival) has not occurred at the reference time and the situation remains open, which sets it to 0 according to the CRIs.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has not yet arrived' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (his hour had not yet come at that moment in the narrative), with negation and temporal specification but no modal, conditional, future, or wish context.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has not yet arrived' contains the auxiliary 'has' (present tense) followed by a past participle, but the full text shows 'had not yet arrived' which uses past-tense 'had', instantiating the TLTS feature per the rule for morphologically past auxiliaries.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has not yet arrived' is an active intransitive verb construction lacking the passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); the subject 'hour' is the semantic agent/experiencer, not a patient undergoing an action.

FullText: » Who are you , « they asked . Jesus replied : » What have I been telling you from the beginning ?
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have been telling' is in passive voice morphology and licenses both an external argument (Jesus as agent) and an internal argument (you as patient/recipient of the telling), satisfying the passive construction criterion for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'telling' licenses an external argument (the speaker 'I') and introduces a direct object ('you'), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the clause contains an overt direct grammatical object introduced by the target verb.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'telling' licenses an internal argument (direct object 'you'), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The present perfect progressive 'have been telling' denotes a durative process of communication extending from a past point ('from the beginning') to the present moment, with an inherent temporal span and directed toward the goal of conveying a message, satisfying the criteria for accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The present perfect progressive 'have been telling' denotes an ongoing durative process unfolding over measurable time ('from the beginning'), not a punctual transition into a new state, and can naturally be modified by 'finish' (e.g., 'finish telling you'), indicating a process phase rather than an achievement boundary.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have been telling' denotes a dynamic, durative situation of ongoing communication that unfolds over time without an inherent endpoint, satisfying the activity criteria of sustained dynamic engagement and homogeneity (any subinterval of telling is still telling).
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have been telling' unfolds over a measurable duration (from the beginning until now) rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, making it a durative activity rather than a semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have been telling' is morphologically marked as progressive aspect ('have been' + present participle), which entails dynamic unfolding and is explicitly incompatible with stative readings per the set_to_0_if criteria.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a situation that has actually occurred (Jesus has been telling them things from the beginning), without any indication of counterfactuality, contrary-to-fact condition, or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have been telling' is not negated; it appears in a direct question without negation markers ('not', 'n't', negative quantifiers, or 'never'), so ClNegPol is not instantiated.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have been telling' denotes communication/discourse where the focus is on the occurrence of repeated telling from the beginning rather than on a resulting state, fitting the experiential perfect category.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have been telling' is a communication verb describing a past interaction without entailing that any information or directive is currently in effect; it merely reports what Jesus has communicated from the beginning without a persisting resulting state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'What have I been telling you from the beginning?' contains the duration adverbial 'from the beginning' indicating a situation (telling) that extends continuously from a past starting point to the reference time, matching the set_to_1_if criteria.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have been telling' has the form 'have been' + -ing (progressive), which profiles the internal temporal development of the situation rather than presenting it as a bounded whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have been telling' appears in a direct question asserting a realized fact about what Jesus has actually been communicating from the beginning, not projecting a hypothetical or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have been telling' includes 'been' followed by a progressive participle ('telling'), which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for progressive perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have been telling' has the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + present participle (not past participle), which marks active progressive aspect rather than passive voice; passive voice requires 'have' + 'been' + past participle (e.g., 'have been told').

FullText: Jesus said : » When you have lifted up impaled the Son of man you will know that I am that person . I do nothing by myself ! I speak what the Father taught me !
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have lifted up' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('you') and an overt direct object ('the Son of man'), satisfying the criterion for a transitive construction with both external and internal arguments.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'lifted up' licenses an external argument (the implied agent 'you' who performs the action of lifting), and the clause contains a direct object ('the Son of man'), making this an agentive transitive construction rather than an unaccusative one.

AktAcc_explanation: The event 'have lifted up (impaled)' involves a directed action toward a defined spatial and result goal—raising the Son of man to an impaled state—which constitutes a durative process culminating in a completed state, satisfying the accomplishment criterion of directed motion/action toward a goal that defines completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have lifted up (impaled)' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the Son of man being elevated/impaled), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials, and represents a definable threshold event rather than an unfolding process.

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have lifted up (impaled)' entails a resulting change of state—the Son of man transitions from a non-impaled to an impaled state—which disqualifies it from being semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have lifted up' denotes a dynamic change-of-state event (raising/impaling) involving internal eventive unfolding and progression toward a bounded endpoint, not a static condition that holds over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'When you have lifted up the Son of man' occurs in an open conditional where the lifting up is presented as a future event that will occur, not as contrary to fact; the apodosis 'you will know that I am' uses future tense, indicating a real conditional relationship rather than a counterfactual one.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have lifted up' is not negated; it appears in a conditional clause ('When you have lifted up...') expressing a future event without negation markers, negative quantifiers, or 'never'.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have lifted up (impaled)' entails a change of state/location (the Son of man being impaled/elevated) whose resulting state is relevant for the subsequent clause 'you will know that I am that person,' making this a narrative progression event rather than an experiential perfect.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have lifted up (impaled)' describes a change of state (the Son of man transitioning to an impaled/elevated state), and the temporal clause 'when you have lifted up' indicates that this resulting state must be achieved before the consequent 'you will know' occurs, entailing that the resulting state holds at the reference time of knowing.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (lifting up/impaling the Son of man) that triggers a subsequent state of knowledge, focusing on the transition and completion of the action rather than a situation holding continuously over time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'when you have lifted up the Son of man' presents a transition event (lifting up/impaling) as a bounded, completed whole that serves as a discrete temporal landmark triggering the consequent clause 'you will know', functioning as a bounded occurrence in narrative sequence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have lifted up' appears in a temporal conditional construction ('When you have lifted up...you will know'), projecting a future event as the condition for a subsequent realization, which instantiates irrealis.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have lifted up' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have lifted up' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'you' as the semantic agent performing the action of lifting; there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle) and the object 'the Son of man' is the semantic patient in object position, not subject position.

FullText: » He sent me and is with me . He has not left me alone . I always do the things that are pleasing to him ! «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'left' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('He') and an overt direct object ('me'), satisfying the condition for ASTrns marking.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'left' licenses an external argument (the subject 'He') as an agent performing directed motion away from the object 'me', and the clause contains an overt direct object 'me', satisfying the set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'left' licenses an internal argument ('me'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'leave' denotes a punctual boundary transition (departure from a state of presence) without a durative process phase; the negation 'has not left' describes the absence of this instantaneous change-of-state rather than a durative process culminating in a result.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'leave' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (departure/separation), naturally modified by time-point adverbials ('at noon'), and does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase, satisfying the criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has not left me alone' denotes a bounded state with an inherent endpoint (the moment of leaving), not a dynamic durative activity; it describes a result state of continuous presence rather than an ongoing action requiring sustained engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has not left me alone' entails a resulting state (remaining in a state of not being alone) and unfolds over a duration rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, thus it is not semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'left' denotes a change-of-state and dynamic event (departure/separation), and the perfect aspect 'has not left' encodes a bounded eventive situation with an internal endpoint, not a static condition holding uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'He has not left me alone' presents a situation as actually occurring in the actual world (a factual state in present perfect), with no indication of counterfactuality, unfulfilled wishes, or conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The auxiliary 'has' is directly negated with 'not' in 'has not left', which instantiates ClNegPol according to the first set_to_1_if condition.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'leave' entails a change of location/state, and 'has not left me alone' asserts that the resulting state (being with me, not abandoned) holds at the reference time, making this a state-focused perfect rather than an experiential one.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'leave' describes a change of state (departure/separation), and the negation 'has not left' entails that the resulting state of non-separation/continued presence persists at the reference time, satisfying the resultative condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'has not left me alone' denotes a continuous state (non-abandonment) that has held from a past point to the reference time, expressing a situation that extends to the present without duration adverbials but with clear temporal continuity characteristic of DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'has not left me alone' is negated with 'not', indicating the transition has not occurred and the situation remains open/unbounded, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for negation blocking perfective construal.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has not left me alone' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world; negation alone does not trigger irrealis, and no modal, conditional, future, or wish context applies.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has not left' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'left' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has not left' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'He' as the semantic agent (doer); it lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: They answered him : » We are Abraham’s seed , and have never been in bondage to any man . How can you say we will be made free ? «
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'been' functions as a copula linking the subject to the predicative complement 'in bondage', rather than introducing an internal argument, thus failing the set_to_1 criteria.
ASUncc_explanation: The target verb 'been in bondage' is a copular construction with 'be' where the sole argument 'we' functions as an internal argument linked to the predicative complement 'in bondage', and no external argument is licensed, matching the copular unaccusative criterion.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'been' (copula) licenses an internal argument ('in bondage'), making this a non-unergative construction that does not meet the ASUnrg criteria.
AktAcc_explanation: The stative predicate 'been in bondage' denotes a state or condition rather than a dynamic durative event with an inherent endpoint; it lacks the process phase and directed culmination required for AktAcc classification.
AktAch_explanation: The phrase 'have never been in bondage' denotes a durative state of existing in a condition over an extended period rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and the perfect aspect with 'never' emphasizes the absence of a state-change event rather than a boundary crossing.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'have never been in bondage' denotes a state or condition (being in bondage) rather than a dynamic, durative activity; it lacks the sustained dynamic engagement required for AktAct and represents a stative situation without inherent unfolding.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'have never been in bondage' describes a state condition (being in bondage) rather than a punctual dynamic event; it entails a resulting state and unfolds over duration, not a single instantaneous occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The present perfect 'have never been in bondage' is morphologically marked with 'have been' (progressive/perfect construction) and denotes a bounded negative state across a time span, but the underlying verb 'be in bondage' involves a change-of-state condition (entering/exiting bondage) rather than a static unchanging situation, making it eventive rather than stative.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a situation understood as having actually occurred in the actual world (the speakers claim they have never been in bondage as a factual statement about their history), without any indication of counterfactuality.
ClNegPol_explanation: The adverb 'never' directly modifies the target gram 'have been in bondage', negating the eventuality of being in bondage according to set_to_1_if rule 3.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'have never been in bondage' is explicitly marked by the frequency adverb 'never' and functions as a claim about the absence of the event in the subject's life history, meeting the set_to_1_if criteria.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'have never been in bondage' uses a stative copula construction ('have been') with a negation ('never'), describing a persistent state rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state; it reports a condition that has held throughout their history without entailing a change of state at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'have never been in bondage' with the negated duration adverbial 'never' denotes a state (not being in bondage) that has held continuously from a past starting point to the reference time, matching the intuition of a situation extending to the present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'have never been in bondage' denotes a stative situation (the state of not being in bondage) without a construed boundary or transition, and the negation with 'never' indicates an unbounded negation of a state rather than a bounded completed event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have never been in bondage' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world as a fact about Abraham's seed, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram includes 'been' followed by a progressive participle structure ('been in bondage'), which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for progressive perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the prepositional phrase 'in bondage', a non-verbal predicate, making 'been' a lexical copula rather than a passive auxiliary; passive voice requires 'been' + past participle of a transitive verb.

FullText: » I speak the things that I have seen and heard when I was with my Father . You also do the things that you heard from your father . «

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have seen' entails a resulting state of knowledge/experience and does not constitute a single punctual occurrence; it describes a durative experiential state rather than an instantaneous dynamic event.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (seeing something), which encodes eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering set_to_0_if criterion 4.

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have seen' reports a past perceptual event without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; it is an experiential perfect describing what the speaker witnessed in the past, not a transition to a state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'I have seen and heard when I was with my Father' describes discrete bounded events (specific things witnessed during a past period) rather than a continuous situation extending to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension to the present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have seen' denotes a stative situation (perception/knowledge state) without a construed boundary or transition event; it describes an unbounded experiential state rather than a bounded completed occurrence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have seen' is a present perfect asserting a realized experience in the actual world, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have seen' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'seen' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have seen' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'I' as the semantic agent (the one doing the seeing) and the object 'things' as the semantic patient; it lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: Jesus said : » If God were your Father you would love me . I came from God ! I have not come from myself . He sent me !
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'come' in 'have not come' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it only takes a prepositional phrase 'from myself' which is not a grammatical object.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument (I) undergoes displacement with no external argument licensed, matching the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a change-of-location event where the subject functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing movement, not an external argument initiating an activity.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a punctual boundary transition (arrival at a location or state) without a durative process phase, matching the achievement category exemplified by 'arrive'.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival at a location/origin), naturally modified by time-point adverbials, and represents a boundary crossing rather than an unfolding process, meeting the criteria for AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'come' entails a specific endpoint (arrival at a destination) and lacks the dynamic durative unfolding required for an activity; it is a bounded, telic event.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have not come' entails a transition into a resulting state (not being present/arrived) and is construed as a durative condition rather than a single punctual occurrence, thus not semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have not come' denotes a change-of-state event (arrival/origin) with an internal boundary marking the onset of a dynamic situation, which is incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have not come from myself' presents a factual statement about what actually occurred (Jesus's actual origin), not a situation contrary to fact, and occurs outside any counterfactual conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The auxiliary 'have' is directly negated with 'not' in 'have not come', which instantiates ClNegPol according to the first set_to_1_if condition.

DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'come' describes a past origin point ('I have not come from myself'), not a current destination state; the speaker is negating an alternative source of origin rather than asserting a persisting resultant location.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'I have not come from myself' describes a negated punctual event (coming) with focus on the source/origin rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the pattern of discrete bounded events.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'have not come' is negated with 'not', indicating the transition has not occurred at the reference time, leaving the situation open rather than presenting it as a bounded completed event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have not come' asserts a realized fact in the actual world (Jesus's origin), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation; negation alone does not trigger irrealis.

AktSemel_explanation: The perfective 'have seen' entails a resulting state of knowledge/experience and does not constitute a single punctual occurrence without consequent result, thus it is not semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (encountering/meeting Abraham), which encodes eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering set_to_0_if criterion 4.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'Have you seen Abraham?' is an embedded interrogative question (indirect question introduced by 'have'), not a counterfactual conditional, and presents an open possibility rather than a situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have seen' appears in an interrogative clause ('Have you seen Abraham?') with no negator directly negating it; the negation 'not' in the sentence applies to 'are' in a separate clause.

DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (seeing Abraham) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'Have you seen Abraham?' is negated with 'not yet' in the preceding context, indicating the transition has not occurred and the situation remains open, which sets it to 0 per the CRIs.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have seen' appears in an interrogative form ('Have you seen Abraham?') that projects a hypothetical or counterfactual scenario (implying the addressee could not have lived long enough to see Abraham), presenting the situation as non-actual rather than asserting a realized fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have seen' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'seen' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have seen' is active voice (subject 'you' is the semantic agent performing the action of seeing), not passive voice; passive voice requires the form 'have been' + past participle, not 'have' + past participle alone.

FullText: The Jews did not believe that he had been blind and then received his sight . So they called his parents .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'be' functions as a copula linking the subject 'he' to the predicative adjective 'blind', introducing no internal argument (direct or indirect object), thus failing the set_to_1 criteria.
ASUncc_explanation: This is a copular clause with 'be' where the sole argument 'he' functions as an internal argument linked to the predicative complement 'blind', and no external argument is licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'blind' denotes a change-of-state condition where the subject functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) experiencing the state, not an external argument (agent/initiator) performing an action.
AktAcc_explanation: The state 'had been blind' denotes a punctual boundary transition into a condition (becoming blind) without a durative process phase inherent to the event itself; it describes a static resultant state rather than an accomplishment with a measurable process leading to completion.
AktAch_explanation: The phrase 'had been blind' denotes a state that unfolds over measurable duration rather than a punctual transition into a new state; blindness is a persistent condition, not a boundary-crossing event.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'had been blind' denotes a state without dynamic unfolding or sustained engagement, lacking the dynamic, durative activity quality required for AktAct; it describes a static condition rather than an ongoing action.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had been blind' entails a resulting state condition (the state of blindness) and unfolds over a measurable duration rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, thus it is not semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had been blind' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect ('had been + -ing form'), which according to the CRIs entails dynamic unfolding and is incompatible with stative readings, triggering set_to_0_if criterion 2.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'had been blind' expresses temporal anteriority within a narrative (he was blind before receiving sight), presenting a factual situation that actually occurred in the world of the text, without any counterfactual marking or indication that the event did not happen.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'did not believe' applies to the matrix verb 'believe', not to the target gram 'had been blind'; the negation does not negate the eventuality of being blind itself.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had been blind' entails a change of state (from sighted to blind) where that resulting state of blindness held at the reference time, making it non-experiential rather than a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'had been blind' is a stative copular construction describing a state rather than encoding a transition; it does not entail a resulting state that holds at the reference time, as the clause indicates the blindness was a prior condition that changed (he 'received his sight').
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'had been blind' denotes a non-terminating state that held continuously from a past starting point until the transition point ('and then received his sight'), satisfying the criterion of a situation extending across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'had been blind' denotes a stative situation (blindness) without a construed boundary or transition; it describes an ongoing state rather than a bounded completed event, and the transition occurs in the coordinate clause 'received his sight' instead.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been blind' is embedded in a reported belief clause ('did not believe that he had been blind'), but it asserts a realized past state of affairs as the content of that belief, not a projected or hypothetical situation; the negation applies to the belief, not to the reality of the blindness itself.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the adjective 'blind' (a non-verbal predicate), making this a copular construction rather than a passive voice construction; passive voice requires 'been' + past participle of a transitive verb.

FullText: The Jews did not believe that he had been blind and then received his sight . So they called his parents .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'received' introduces an overt grammatical subject (he) and an overt direct object (his sight), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns marking.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'received' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'he' who actively receives/obtains his sight as a direct object), making it transitive and not unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'received' licenses an internal argument ('his sight'), which is the direct object, so it does not instantiate ASUnrg.

AktAch_explanation: The verb phrase 'received his sight' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from blindness to sight), lacks measurable internal duration, and naturally accepts a specific time-point adverbial, meeting the criteria for an achievement.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'received his sight' entails a specific, inherent endpoint (the moment sight was regained) and a defined result state (having sight), making it a bounded accomplishment rather than a dynamic, durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'received his sight' entails a transition into a new resulting state (from blindness to sightedness), which disqualifies it from being semelfactive according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'received his sight' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness/ability (change-of-state from blindness to sight), involving dynamic change and an event boundary, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a situation that actually occurred in the actual world (he did receive his sight), and the past perfect 'had been blind' and 'received his sight' express temporal anteriority within a narrative without any counterfactual marking.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'did not believe' applies to the matrix verb 'believe', not to the target gram 'had received'; the target gram itself is not negated and describes a realized event within the scope of the belief.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'received his sight' entails a change of state (from blind to sighted) where the resulting state of being able to see persists at the reference time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for verbs entailing change of state.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'received his sight' describes a change of state (from blind to sighted), and the context indicates this resulting state persists at the reference time, as the man can now see.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (receiving sight) representing a transition into a new state, not a situation holding continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'received his sight' is a transition event (change from blindness to sight) construed as a completed whole, and the clause functions as a discrete bounded event in the narrative sequence introduced by 'then'.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had received his sight' is embedded in a reported belief clause ('The Jews did not believe that...') but asserts a realized fact about what actually happened in the world, not a projected or hypothetical situation; negation of belief does not make the embedded clause irrealis.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, forming a past perfect construction ('had received'), which instantiates TLTS as it encodes past tense specification beyond the simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had received his sight' is active voice (subject 'he' is the semantic agent performing the action of receiving), not passive voice; passive voice requires the morphological pattern 'have/had + been + past participle', which is absent here.

FullText: The parents said these things because they feared the Jews . The Jews had agreed that if any man should confess him to be Christ , he should be put out of the synagogue .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had agreed' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('The Jews') and an overt internal argument (the finite clause 'that if any man should confess him to be Christ, he should be put out of the synagogue'), which functions as the object of agreement.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'agreed' licenses an external argument (the Jews as agent/initiator) and introduces an internal argument (the subordinate clause 'that if any man should confess him to be Christ...'), making it transitive rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'agreed' licenses an internal argument (the finite clause 'that if any man should confess him to be Christ, he should be put out of the synagogue'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'agree' denotes a punctual mental transition into a state of consensus without a durative process phase; it is a boundary transition into agreement rather than a gradual change-of-state culminating in a result.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had agreed' denotes a punctual transition into a new state of mutual commitment or consensus, representing a definable boundary point where the Jews crossed from disagreement/no agreement into agreement, satisfying the achievement criterion of a near-instantaneous change-of-state event.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had agreed' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of a mutual decision with a defined result state (the agreement itself), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds over time.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had agreed' entails a transition into a resulting state (a state of agreement/commitment) and does not constitute a single punctual occurrence without consequent result, thus it is not semelfactive.
